source_paper: Experts’ recommendations for the management of adult patients with cardiogenic shock
author: Nadia Aissaoui et al.
pearl: For patients with cardiogenic shock, utilize a multidisciplinary 'Shock Team' consisting of at least a heart failure specialist, an interventional cardiologist, and an intensivist or cardiac anesthesiologist to facilitate rapid SCAI staging and collaborative decision-making regarding temporary mechanical circulatory support (t-MCS).

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Staphylococcus aureus (especially MRSA) has surpassed viridans group streptococci as the most common and virulent pathogen in developed countries, causing rapid valvular destruction, perivalvular abscesses, and systemic embolization.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Under the 2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria, a definitive clinical diagnosis of infective endocarditis requires the presence of either 2 major criteria, 1 major and 3 minor criteria, or 5 minor criteria.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: To satisfy the major microbiological criterion, typical pathogens (such as S. aureus, S. lugdunensis, E. faecalis, VGS, and HACEK) must be isolated from at least 2 separate blood culture sets, whereas less common pathogens require isolation from at least 3 separate sets.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Major microbiological criteria for intracellular pathogens are met with a positive PCR for Coxiella burnetii, Bartonella spp., or Tropheryma whipplei, an IgG anti-phase I titer for C. burnetii > 1:800, or an IFA IgG titer >= 1:800 for B. henselae or B. quintana.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: When using 18F-FDG PET/CT to identify abnormal metabolic activity as a major imaging criterion, the prosthetic valve must have been implanted for at least 3 months to avoid false-positive results from physiological postoperative inflammation.

source_paper: JCS 2026 Guideline on the Management of Infective Endocarditis
author: Chisato Izumi et al.
pearl: Glomerulonephritis qualifies as an immunological minor criterion for infective endocarditis and is clinically defined by acute kidney injury associated with hematuria, proteinuria, red cell casts, or hypocomplementemia.

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: VExUS is an ultrasound protocol integrating IVC diameter (≥2 cm) and Doppler flow patterns of hepatic, portal, and intrarenal veins to assess fluid tolerance.

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: In cardiac patients, VExUS grade 2 or higher significantly predicts AKI (pooled OR 4.44) and mortality (pooled OR 3.17).

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: For general critically ill patients, **only VExUS grade 3** is significantly associated with increased risk of all-cause mortality (OR 2.42).

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: VExUS should be interpreted as a marker of 'reduced fluid tolerance' rather than solely volume overload.

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: When VExUS is grade 2 or 3, clinicians must carefully consider the risks associated with further fluid administration.

source_paper: Diagnostic and Prognostic Value of the Venous Excess Ultrasound Grading System: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
author: Peter Klompmaker et al.
pearl: VExUS grades are useful for detecting elevated RAP (>10 mm Hg), showing a sensitivity of 87% and specificity of 90% when grade 2 or higher is present.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: Never administer fluids solely to 'normalize' a low CVP; instead, monitor the change in CVP during a bolus: if it rises by > 3 mmHg without increased cardiac output/stroke volume, stop infusion immediately.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: In septic shock or cardiorenal syndrome, target maintaining renal perfusion gradient (MAP - CVP) rather than normalizing CVP to maximize kidney function and avoid positive fluid balances.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: Integrate CVP monitoring with bedside echocardiography and VExUS Doppler grading to create a comprehensive 'congestion profile' before initiating any fluid removal or aggressive resuscitation.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: CVP must be measured at end-expiration (or zeroed accordingly) to minimize the overestimation of preload caused by mechanical ventilation and PEEP.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: The Mean Perfusion Pressure (MPP = MAP - CVP) is a critical determinant of organ perfusion; elevated CVP directly narrows this gradient, impairing renal function even if MAP is maintained.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: A rapid, disproportionate rise in CVP (e.g., delta CVP > 2-3 mmHg) during a fluid challenge without increased stroke volume indicates limited fluid tolerance and requires immediate cessation of fluids.

source_paper: Central Venous Pressure Revisited: Physiology, Pitfalls, Misconceptions, and Modern Clinical Interpretation in Critical Care
author: Cesare Biuzzi et al.
pearl: The historical goal-directed target for static CVP (8-12 mmHg or 12-15 mmHg in mechanically ventilated patients) has been abandoned by major guidelines due to lack of survival benefit in septic shock.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: Mandatory neurovascular checks must be performed pre- and post-transport for IABP patients to monitor for limb ischemia and radial pulse changes.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: For IABP timing errors (early/late inflation or deflation), 'Automode' should be used for correction rather than manual adjustment.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: During CPR, continue IABP support and switch to pressure trigger mode if the ECG signal is unreliable due to artifact.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: A 'Rapid Gas Loss' alarm indicates possible balloon rupture; immediately stop the pump and prepare for catheter removal.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: The IABP battery life is typically 2-2.25 hours, necessitating that backup power sources are always available during transport.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: Maintain the head of bed at less than 30 degrees (<30°) during IABP transport to prevent femoral catheter kinking or bleeding.

source_paper: The Intra-aortic Balloon Pump: A Focused Review of Physiology, Transport Logistics, Mechanics, and Complications
author: Lauren E. Gillespie, MD et al.
pearl: The device functions via counterpulsation: inflating in diastole (T-P interval) to increase coronary perfusion pressure and deflating in systole (QRS-T interval) to reduce left ventricular afterload.

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: Prioritize 'sports-mode' ECMO for stable HLTx candidates needing longer bridge times to maintain ambulation, utilizing surgical graft implantation into the subclavian artery and internal jugular vein.

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: When using VA-ECMO in patients with LV failure, monitor for LV distention and pulmonary congestion; decompression methods include atrial septostomy, IABP, Impella, or direct LV venting.

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: In Eisenmenger patients, assess bleeding risk due to extensive collateralization before initiating ECMO.

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: Multidisciplinary team involvement is mandatory for selecting the appropriate ECMO configuration based on patient pathophysiology (e.g., LV/RV failure, shunt dynamics).

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: For Eisenmenger patients with an ASD, a dual-lumen cannula can be positioned to direct oxygenated blood across the defect to improve systemic oxygenation.

source_paper: Bridging strategies for heart-lung transplantation: Current concepts and mechanical support modalities
author: K. Hoetzenecker, J.W. Stokes, M. Bacchetta, and O. Mercier
pearl: The V-PA ECMO (Oxy-RVAD) is currently not recommended as a standard bridging protocol for HLTx due to limited data.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: Avoid routine preoperative TTE in asymptomatic, stable patients due to lack of evidence for improved clinical outcomes.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: Utilize Focused Cardiac Ultrasound (FOCUS) as a targeted tool for specific clinical questions (e.g., suspected heart failure or valvular disease) rather than broad screening.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: Prioritize clinical assessment (symptoms, functional capacity, biomarkers like NT-proBNP) over indiscriminate imaging when assessing patients for non-cardiac surgery.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: The ESC 2022 guidelines recommend preoperative echocardiography Class I for symptomatic patients, genetic cardiomyopathy, or high-risk non-cardiac surgery with poor functional capacity/high NT-proBNP.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: The ESC 2022 guidelines suggest FOCUS (Class IIb) for intermediate-risk non-cardiac surgery if the patient has poor functional capacity or high NT-proBNP to avoid surgical delay.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: The AHA/ACC 2024 guidelines recommend preoperative echocardiography Class I for patients with new dyspnea, physical exam findings of heart failure, or suspected severe valvular disease.

source_paper: Echocardiography before non-cardiac surgery: current knowledge, guideline recommendations, and clinical evidence – a narrative review
author: Viktoria Mertin, Alexandra Stroda, Tobias Bruns, Giovanna Lurati Buse
pearl: The AHA/ACC 2024 guidelines suggest FOCUS (Class IIb) for known LV dysfunction (stable) or previously documented LV dysfunction without TTE in >12 months.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Interventions for sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy (SICM) should target the first 24-48 hours of septic shock to stabilize the gut barrier.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Early enteral nutrition with fiber is prioritized in sepsis to support beneficial short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) production.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Clinical trials for SICM should adopt phenotype-enriched designs and utilize cardiac-specific endpoints such as LVEF recovery or vasopressor-free days.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Avoid indiscriminate use of probiotics in hemodynamically unstable patients due to potential risks of microbial translocation.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Butyrate is a protective SCFA that activates GPR43/GPR41/GPR109A and reduces cytokine storms, suggesting cardioprotective potential in sepsis.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Indole-3-propionic acid (IPA) protects against cardiomyocyte injury by activating AhR and inhibiting NF-kB/NLRP3 pathways.

source_paper: The gut–heart axis in sepsis-induced cardiomyopathy: pathophysiology, microbial metabolites, and cardiovascular insights
author: Zhaokai Li and Guoqiang Zhang
pearl: Activation of FXR and TGR5 using bile acids (e.g., Obeticholic acid or UDCA) shows promise in preserving mitochondrial integrity and reducing reactive oxygen species (ROS).

source_paper: The Effect of Restrictive vs Liberal Blood Transfusion Strategy on Subsequent Myocardial Infarction Type
author: Andrew P. DeFilippis et al.
pearl: In patients with MI and anemia, the choice between a restrictive or liberal blood transfusion strategy does not differentially affect the risk of subsequent Myocardial Infarction (MI), regardless of whether it is Type 1 or Type 2.

source_paper: Magnesium Sulfate to Prevent Perioperative Atrial Fibrillation in Cardiac Surgery: A Randomized Clinical Trial
author: Manon Meerman, MD et al.
pearl: Routine prophylactic IV magnesium sulfate targeting 1.5-2.0 mmol/L does not prevent postoperative atrial fibrillation (POAF) in cardiac surgery patients and may increase vasopressor requirements.

source_paper: Magnesium Sulfate to Prevent Perioperative Atrial Fibrillation in Cardiac Surgery: A Randomized Clinical Trial
author: Manon Meerman, MD et al.
pearl: Aggressive magnesium supplementation may cause vasodilation, potentially increasing the requirement for vasopressors like noradrenaline in the early postoperative period (40.2% vs 28.6%; p=0.05).

source_paper: Magnesium Sulfate to Prevent Perioperative Atrial Fibrillation in Cardiac Surgery: A Randomized Clinical Trial
author: Manon Meerman, MD et al.
pearl: The primary focus of electrolyte management should be correcting hypomagnesemia (< 1.0 mmol/L) rather than aiming for prophylactic hypermagnesemia.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: For stable LGI bleed patients, calculate Oakland Score (<= 8 for safe ED discharge) and SHA2PE Score (<= 1 for low intervention risk).

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: Unstable patients require immediate hemostatic resuscitation using whole blood or a balanced 1:1:1 transfusion protocol; TXA is contraindicated due to increased VTE/mortality risk.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: For unstable patients, perform emergent arterial-phase CTA; if positive, target interventional radiology 'needle-stick' within 1 hour. Bypass CTA for direct angiography in profound hemorrhagic shock.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: Stable patients or those with negative CTA should undergo colonoscopy within 24 hours after PEG bowel preparation.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: In patients on DAPT, continue aspirin and temporarily hold the secondary antiplatelet agent (e.g., P2Y12 inhibitor).

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: Reverse warfarin with 4-factor PCC; restart warfarin only 7 to 14 days post-hemostasis.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: For small bowel hemorrhage when upper endoscopy and colonoscopy are negative, video capsule endoscopy (VCE) is the diagnostic modality of choice and should be performed within 48 hours.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: In profound hemorrhagic shock requiring continuous active transfusion, bypass CTA in favor of immediate catheter-based visceral angiography to avoid delay.

source_paper: Evidence-based, cost-effective management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding. An algorithm of the journal of trauma and acute care surgery emergency general surgery algorithms work group
pearl: Visceral angiography following positive CTA should be performed within 1 hour (needle-stick time < 1 hour) due to its time-sensitive nature.

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: Early VA-ECMO initiation is associated with a 26% reduction in short-term mortality (OR=0.74, 95% CI 0.58-0.96, P=0.02) compared to delayed initiation.

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: Pre-revascularization VA-ECMO initiation showed a strong mortality benefit (OR=0.40, 95% CI 0.23-0.68, P<0.01).

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: Early VA-ECMO does not significantly increase the risk of neurological (OR=0.67), bleeding (OR=0.83), or ischemic complications.

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: Long-term mortality benefit from early VA-ECMO was not observed (OR=0.63, 95% CI 0.39-1.02, P=0.06).

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: The benefit of early VA-ECMO may be attenuated in patients with SCAI shock stage E (actual or impending circulatory collapse); optimal timing may be better guided by shock severity stages B-D.

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: Future research should focus on identifying optimal candidates using the SCAI shock classification rather than relying solely on time-based definitions for VA-ECMO initiation.

source_paper: Timing of veno-arterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation in cardiogenic shock: A systematic review and meta-analysis
author: Xiang-kun Yuan et al.
pearl: The ECMO-CS randomized trial found no benefit from immediate routine VA-ECMO, suggesting a need to identify specific patients who benefit from early intervention versus a judicious rescue strategy.

author: Guglielmo Imbriaco, RN, MSN, Lorenzo Gamberini, MD, et al.

source_paper: The burden of hepatic encephalopathy and the use of albumin as a potential treatment
author: Jasmohan S. Bajaj, Enrico Pompili, Paolo Caraceni

source_paper: The burden of hepatic encephalopathy and the use of albumin as a potential treatment
author: Jasmohan S. Bajaj, Enrico Pompili, Paolo Caraceni

source_paper: Chronic hepatitis B in 2025: diagnosis, treatment and future directions

source_paper: Chronic hepatitis B in 2025: diagnosis, treatment and future directions

source_paper: Chronic hepatitis B in 2025: diagnosis, treatment and future directions

source_paper: Heart Failure Occurring in the Perinatal Period: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association

source_paper: Heart Failure Occurring in the Perinatal Period: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association

source_paper: Heart Failure Occurring in the Perinatal Period: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Natriuretic peptide cutoffs: BNP <100 pg/mL and NT-proBNP <128 pg/mL retain high negative predictive value for excluding HF in pregnancy.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: SBP is diagnosed with ascitic fluid PMN count > 250/mm3.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Secondary prophylaxis for SBP should start immediately after acute antibiotic course completion (e.g., co-trimoxazole 960 mg daily).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Initial AKI management requires suspending diuretics, beta-blockers, and NSAIDs; crystalloid boluses of 250 mL can be used to target UO > 0.5 mL/kg/hr.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HDU escalation for AKI is needed if there is persistent hypotension, UO < 0.5 mL/kg/hr, rising lactate, or respiratory failure after 6 hours.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For HRS-AKI, pharmacotherapy includes Terlipressin (2-8 mg daily, titrated) combined with 20% albumin (100 mL BID).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: All upper GI bleeds in cirrhotic patients should be treated as AVH until proven otherwise.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Pre-endoscopy pharmacotherapy for suspected AVH includes Terlipressin (2 mg IV bolus, then 1-2 mg QDS) or Octreotide (50 mcg IV bolus, then 50 mcg/hr infusion if terlipressin is contraindicated).

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Empirical IV antibiotics and IV erythromycin (250 mg) are recommended pre-endoscopy for AVH.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: HE management requires titrating lactulose to achieve 2-3 soft stools daily; Rifaximin is reserved for secondary prevention of recurrent/chronic HE and does not acutely improve active episodes.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For non-tense ascites or peripheral edema, diuretics are used (Spironolactone 100 mg daily + Furosemide 40 mg daily), titrated every 72 hours to target 0.5-1.0 kg daily weight loss.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Malnutrition requires a high-protein diet (1.5 g/kg/day) with small, frequent meals and aggressive replacement of potassium, magnesium, and phosphate.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: VTE prophylaxis with tinzaparin is required for all patients with a platelet count > 50 x 10^9/L in the absence of active bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: Corticosteroid therapy (Prednisolone 40 mg daily) is indicated for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (AH), determined by prognostic scores like Maddrey's Discriminant Function or MELD, after excluding active infection and GI bleeding.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: If a patient with severe AH is a non-responder (Lille score > 0.45) at Day 4-7, corticosteroid therapy should be stopped immediately.

source_paper: Management of acute decompensated cirrhosis
author: Dina Mansour
pearl: For urgent liver transplant assessment in ARLD, consider patients < 60 years old with 3-organ failure, no contraindications, and at least 3 months of documented abstinence.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Implement routine FIB-4 screening for high-risk populations (T2D, obesity, metabolic syndrome), integrating it into annual diabetes reviews.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Utilize a two-tier screening pathway: FIB-4 first; if >= 1.3, reflex to ELF or Transient Elastography before hepatology referral.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: MASLD diagnosis requires objective hepatic steatosis plus at least one cardiometabolic criterion (e.g., BMI >= 25 kg/m2 for general population; HbA1c >= 39 mmol/L).

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: For advanced fibrosis risk stratification: FIB-4 index > 2.67 requires hepatology referral; intermediate (1.3-2.67) needs secondary testing (ELF/TE).

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Lifestyle interventions are foundational: achieving >= 10% weight loss is required to resolve MASH and regress fibrosis.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Pharmacological agents for MASH include Resmetirom (THR-beta agonist), Semaglutide (GLP-1 RA), which have demonstrated MASH resolution and fibrosis improvement.

source_paper: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease
author: Catherine Hsu et al.
pearl: Statins are safe and essential for primary/secondary prevention in MASLD patients due to the critical role of cardiovascular protection.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Restrict paracetamol use to 2–3 g/24h in stable cirrhosis; for acute end-of-life pain, subcutaneous morphine 2.5 mg hourly is appropriate, but substitute with fentanyl if eGFR is reduced.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: For patients discharged after hepatic encephalopathy (HE), a clear lactulose titration plan targeting 2–3 soft stools/day and consideration of rifaximin are essential components of care.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Implement the Bristol Screening Tool on acute medical admission: a score > 2 suggests high risk of death within 12 months and mandates immediate supportive/palliative referral and Advance Care Planning discussion while cognitive capacity is retained.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: For diuretic-resistant ascites, transition to scheduled, planned paracentesis pathways (e.g., indwelling peritoneal catheters) rather than relying on emergency department presentations.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Early palliative care referral in HCC is associated with fewer inpatient bed days, lower rates of in-hospital death, and reduced healthcare costs compared to standard care.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Sarcopenia prevalence correlates strongly with frailty and poor clinical outcomes; early dietician involvement for nutritional optimization improves QOL and reduces covert HE symptoms.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: Advance Care Planning (ACP) must be initiated early in the course of HE while the patient retains decision-making capacity due to fluctuating cognitive impairment.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: When ascites is refractory to diuretics, planned Large-Volume Paracentesis (LVP) reduces emergency inpatient days and in-hospital death risk compared to reactive paracentesis.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: In patients with concurrent renal impairment or hepatorenal syndrome, morphine must be avoided or replaced with fentanyl when managing pain due to risk of toxic metabolite accumulation.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: The Bristol Screening Tool for decompensated cirrhosis recommends referral if the patient has a Child-Pugh Grade C, two or more liver-related admissions in 6 months, ongoing alcohol use (ArLD), is unsuitable for LT workup, or has WHO Performance Status 3–4.

source_paper: Palliative care in advanced liver disease
author: Christopher Cussen et al.
pearl: The Supportive and Palliative Care Indicators Tool (SPICT) identifies cirrhosis patients not suitable for transplant who have experienced diuretic-resistant ascites, HE, HRS, SBP, or recurrent variceal bleeds in the past year.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Monoclonal Antibodies (MAb) showed a significant reduction in all-cause mortality (RR 0.92; 95% CrI 0.84 to 0.99) in adult sepsis patients.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Corticosteroids and G/M-CSF did not demonstrate a survival benefit in sepsis according to this network meta-analysis.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Ulinastatin (UTI) acts by inhibiting the NF-kB pathway, stabilizing lysosomal membranes, protecting the endothelial glycocalyx, and reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-alpha and IL-1beta.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: The combination of Ulinastatin (UTI) and Thymosin-alpha1 targets both hyperinflammation (via UTI) and immunosuppression (via Ta1), suggesting a synergistic approach.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: PUFAs modulate lipid raft composition and promote specialized pro-resolving mediators (e.g., resolvins, protectins), contributing to their anti-inflammatory effects in sepsis.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of immunomodulatory therapies for sepsis: a systematic review and network meta-analysis
author: Luo R, et al.
pearl: Thymosin-alpha1 enhances T-cell differentiation and proliferation while promoting Th1 cytokine responses (IFN-gamma, IL-2) and improving antigen presentation via dendritic cell activation.

author: Zhou L. et al.
pearl: Transition from intermittent bolus (IB) to prolonged infusion strategies (EI or CI) for beta-lactam antibiotics is recommended to optimize clinical cure rates in adult ICU patients with severe infections.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.
pearl: Extended infusion (EI, 2-4 hours) should be preferred over continuous infusion (CI, 24 hours) due to superior practical feasibility and fewer operational challenges in the ICU setting.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.
pearl: Prolonged infusion strategies (EI or CI) are aimed at maintaining sustained serum and tissue concentrations above the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (%T > MIC), addressing subtherapeutic levels caused by altered pharmacokinetics in critically ill patients.

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.
pearl: The efficacy of beta-lactam antibiotics is time-dependent, determined by the percentage of the dosing interval that free drug concentration remains above the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (%T > MIC).

source_paper: Comparative efficacy and safety of extended versus continuous infusion of beta-lactam antibiotics for severe infection: a network meta-analysis of randomized trials
author: Zhou L. et al.
pearl: EI was associated with a borderline significant reduction in hospital stay compared to IB (MD -3.49 days; 95% CI -6.79 to -0.08), suggesting potential benefit over intermittent bolus dosing.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: When planning or delivering Remote Ischemic Conditioning (RIC), avoid propofol for induction or maintenance of anesthesia; use volatile anesthetics or alternative agents to preserve the conditioning effect.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: In patients within 12 hours of septic shock, consider the RECO-Sepsis protocol (4 cycles of 5-min upper limb ischemia, repeated at 12 and 24 hours) as a safe, low-cost adjunctive therapy.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: For moderate acute ischemic stroke patients not receiving acute reperfusion therapies, consider bilateral upper limb RIC (5 cycles twice daily for 10-14 days) to improve 90-day functional recovery.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: The standard protocol for RIC involves 3 to 5 cycles of 5 minutes of cuff inflation followed by 5 minutes of cuff deflation; inflation pressure should be 180 to 200 mmHg or 50 mmHg above SBP.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: In septic shock, the RECO-Sepsis trial showed a significant survival benefit with RIC (adjusted Hazard Ratio 0.59, 95% CI 0.35-0.99, p = 0.049) when administered within 12 hours of onset.

source_paper: Remote ischemic conditioning in intensive care: From bench to bedside
author: Sarah Benghanem et al.
pearl: Propofol actively abrogates the protective signaling pathways of RIC (specifically interfering with STAT5 activation), which explains negative outcomes in trials where it was used for anesthesia maintenance.

pearl: In patients with suspected sepsis/septic shock, obtain lactate levels immediately; persistent elevation (> 2 mmol/L) suggests ongoing tissue hypoperfusion requiring aggressive resuscitation.

source_paper: 2026 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ACEP/CHEST/SCAI/SHM/SIR/SVM/SVN Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism in Adults: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines
pearl: Source control (e.g., surgical debridement of infected necrotic tissue) remains a cornerstone of septic shock management and must be aggressively pursued.

source_paper: 2026 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ACEP/CHEST/SCAI/SHM/SIR/SVM/SVN Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism in Adults: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines
pearl: Mechanical ventilation initiation in septic patients should follow standard lung protective strategies (low tidal volume, PEEP titration) unless contraindications exist.

source_paper: 2026 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ACEP/CHEST/SCAI/SHM/SIR/SVM/SVN Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Acute Pulmonary Embolism in Adults: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines
pearl: Consider prophylactic antibiotics for high-risk surgical procedures or invasive devices based on institutional guidelines and specific risk factors, rather than routine use.

author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Heatstroke must be distinguished by core temperature >40 degrees C, CNS dysfunction, and multi-organ involvement.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: For heatstroke management, initiate rapid active cooling within 30 minutes of presentation, preferably via ice-water (1-5 degrees C) or cold-water (9-12 degrees C) immersion.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Do not routinely administer paracetamol, NSAIDs, or salicylates for temperature reduction in heatstroke due to ineffectiveness and risk of exacerbating organ injury.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Aggressively manage complications of heat-related illness, including rhabdomyolysis (requiring aggressive hydration and urine alkalinization), DIC, AKI, and cerebral edema.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Review and adjust medications that impair thermoregulation or worsen dehydration during heatwaves, specifically listing diuretics, anticholinergics, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, and antidepressants.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Utilize hematocrit and serum osmolality to assess subclinical volume depletion, and monitor thyroid function and cortisol levels for endocrine-mediated thermoregulatory stress.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is the primary driver of heatwave-related mortality; a global meta-analysis showed a 12% increase in CVD-related mortality risk during heatwaves (RR 1.12).

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Heat exposure is associated with a 26.0 mL decrease in FEV1, a 35.8 mL/s decrease in peak expiratory flow (PEF), and a 23.4 mL decrease in FVC in asthmatic adults.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Heatstroke-associated AKI can present as exertional (with rhabdomyolysis) or epidemic (characterized by acute interstitial nephritis with urinary leukocytosis, hematuria, and tubulointerstitial damage).

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Dehydration increases the risk of UTI by 7.3% per 10 degrees F elevation in warm-season temperature.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Heat stress stimulates the HPA axis, increasing cortisol release proportional to core temperature, while simultaneously suppressing TSH, T4, and T3 levels.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Hyperthermia compromises the blood-brain barrier (BBB) by damaging cerebrovascular endothelial cells, leading to serum protein leakage and cerebral edema.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Extreme heat damages intestinal epithelial tight junction proteins, increasing gut permeability and allowing endotoxin (LPS) translocation into systemic circulation.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Thermal stress induces a dominant pro-inflammatory response (elevated IL-1beta, IL-6, IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha) that overwhelms anti-inflammatory mediators like IL-1Ra and IL-10.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: In pregnancy, heat exposure elevates risks of preterm birth (OR 1.16), stillbirth (OR 1.05), and low birth weight (OR 1.13).

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: For community adaptation, active cooling via cold-water or ice-water immersion is the gold standard for heatstroke management.

source_paper: Effects of extreme heat on physiology, morbidity, and mortality under climate change: mechanisms and clinical implications
author: Jiayu Xu et al.
pearl: Low-income urban neighborhoods can experience temperatures up to 5 degrees C higher than wealthier areas due to dense housing and lack of green space (Urban Heat Islands).

author: Mahan Sadjadi et al.

source_paper: Implementation of the kidney protection strategy in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury – a multi-center prospective cohort study
author: Mahan Sadjadi et al.

source_paper: Implementation of the kidney protection strategy in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury – a multi-center prospective cohort study
author: Mahan Sadjadi et al.
pearl: The KPS requires hourly documentation of urine output; a breach is defined as a gap exceeding one consecutive hour.

source_paper: Implementation of the kidney protection strategy in critically ill patients with acute kidney injury – a multi-center prospective cohort study
author: Mahan Sadjadi et al.
pearl: Functional hemodynamic monitoring should be assessed using methods like echocardiography or transpulmonary thermodilution at least three times daily.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.
pearl: When performing CRRT in ABI patients, use Regional Citrate Anticoagulation (RCA) and monitor ionized calcium/acid-base; avoid systemic heparin due to intracranial hemorrhage risk.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.
pearl: Augmented Renal Clearance (ARC), defined as CrCl > 130 mL/min/1.73 m2, is common in neurocritical care and mandates therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for renal-eliminated drugs like vancomycin and levetiracetam.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.
pearl: Hyperosmolar therapy caution: Limit mannitol use if serum osmolality exceeds 320 mOsm/L.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.

source_paper: Acute kidney injury in neurocritical care
author: Faeq Husain-Syed et al.
pearl: In neuro-ICU patients, screen for ARC and increase doses of renal-eliminated drugs based on TDM to prevent underdosing.

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know
author: Alex B. Valadka et al.
pearl: Target Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) for cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is 60-70 mmHg.

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know
author: Alex B. Valadka et al.

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Current management of traumatic intracranial hypertension: What you need to know

source_paper: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Fluid Management: Finding the Perfect Balance

source_paper: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Fluid Management: Finding the Perfect Balance
author: Sbaraini Zernini et al.

source_paper: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Fluid Management: Finding the Perfect Balance

source_paper: Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome and Fluid Management: Finding the Perfect Balance

author: Sbaraini Zernini et al.

source_paper: Acute respiratory distress mimickers: a practical approach for intensivists

source_paper: Imaging in ARDS: physiology-guided decisions in the AI era

author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: Weight-Based Dosing options include 30 mg BID for 50-59 kg; 40 mg BID for 60-99 kg; and 50 mg BID for >= 100 kg.

source_paper: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: For stable, non-operative TBI patients meeting Parkland's Modified Berne-Norwood Low-Risk Criteria (e.g., SDH <= 8 mm, EDH <= 8 mm, isolated SAH/IVH), initiate enoxaparin 24 hours post-injury.

source_paper: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: For stable, non-operative TBI patients failing low-risk criteria or showing progression at 24 hours, repeat CT should be performed at 72 hours before considering initiation of enoxaparin.

source_paper: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: Chemoprophylaxis for all stable blunt Solid Organ Injuries (SOI) should be initiated within 48 hours of admission once hemostasis is achieved and active bleeding is absent.

source_paper: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: In pregnancy, LMWH is the preferred agent over UFH due to lower risks of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) and osteoporosis.

source_paper: Prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism in trauma: What you need to know
author: Michelle Lippincott et al.
pearl: Routine screening ultrasound for DVT in all trauma patients is not cost-effective; weekly duplex ultrasound should be reserved only for high-risk patients who cannot receive or maintain pharmacologic prophylaxis.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Place a distal perfusion cannula in the superficial femoral artery of the cannulated limb for all ECPR patients to prevent lower limb ischemia.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Maintain strict normothermia (36.0–37.5 °C) and avoid fever (>37.5 °C) using the ECMO circuit’s integrated heater‑cooler unit.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Delay formal neuroprognostication until at least 72 hours after arrest, using multimodal assessment and avoiding early neurological findings for decisions.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Target a right radial arterial PaO₂ of 75–100 mmHg and a PaCO₂ of 35–45 mmHg, and avoid rapid PaCO₂ drops >20 mmHg within 24 hours.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Consider mechanical left‑ventricular unloading (IABP, Impella) when the aortic valve remains closed, LV dilation worsens, pulmonary edema progresses, or pulse pressure falls below 5 mmHg.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Perform a routine head‑to‑pelvis CT pan‑scan early after resuscitation to identify CPR‑related injuries or the underlying cause of arrest.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Use a restrictive transfusion strategy in non‑bleeding ECPR patients, targeting hemoglobin ≥80 g/L and platelet count ≥50 000/mL.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Urgently perform cardiac catheterization in 100 % of ECPR patients after initiation unless a clear non‑cardiac cause of arrest is documented.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Minimize sedation with short‑acting agents (propofol, dexmedetomidine) to facilitate early neuroprognostication once hemodynamic stability is achieved.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Consider empiric post‑cannulation antibiotic therapy based on individual infectious risk profiles and prolonged CPR exposure.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Apply lung‑protective ventilation with tidal volume <6 mL/kg predicted body weight.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Use PEEP >10 cm H₂O in cases of pulmonary edema, atelectasis, or LV dilatation.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Avoid extreme hyperoxemia (PaO₂ >300 mmHg); titrate FiO₂ to achieve PaO₂ 75–100 mmHg or SpO₂ 94–98 %.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Maintain PaCO₂ 35–45 mmHg and prevent rapid decreases >20 mmHg within 24 hours by gradual sweep gas titration.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Simultaneously monitor right radial arterial blood gases and post‑oxygenator blood gases to detect dual circulation and assess membrane lung performance.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Target an initial MAP >65 mmHg; if MAP exceeds 80 mmHg with LV distension or pulmonary edema, initiate active afterload reduction.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Titrate ECMO flow to maintain systemic perfusion guided by lactate clearance, urine output, and echocardiography.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Aim for a pulse pressure ≥10 mmHg to ensure native LV ejection.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Prioritize consistent aortic valve opening; persistent closure requires intervention.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Perform a comprehensive echocardiogram within 24 hours of ICU admission and repeat daily, monitoring for pericardial effusion.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: If anticoagulation is temporarily held due to life‑threatening hemorrhage, maintain ECMO blood flow >2.0 L/min to reduce circuit thrombosis risk.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Perform duplex ultrasound screening for DVT after decannulation.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Weaning trials should reduce ECMO flows to 1.5–2.0 L/min while monitoring hemodynamics and native cardiac function via echocardiography.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: If a patient fails to wean from ECMO, evaluate for advanced therapies (VAD, transplantation) or transition to palliative care.

source_paper: Recommendations in Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Post Resuscitation Care Via An International, Modified Delphi Approach
pearl: Routine therapeutic anticoagulation is not indicated after decannulation unless a separate clinical indication exists.

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial

pearl: Delayed attainment of the randomized lower-bound MAP target (≥12 h) is associated with higher 90‑day mortality compared with attainment within <1 h (adjusted OR 1.48, 95 % CI 1.11–1.98; p = 0.008).

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.
pearl: No significant association with 90‑day mortality was found for time‑to‑target MAP categories of 1–3 h, 3–6 h, or 6–12 h.

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.
pearl: In patients who achieved the target MAP within 24 h, each additional hour of time to target MAP was not significantly associated with 90‑day mortality (adjusted OR 1.025, 95 % CI 0.972–1.082; p = 0.362).

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.
pearl: Prolonged time to target MAP (≥12 h or unreached) versus early attainment was associated with a 3.24‑fold higher hazard of 90‑day mortality (adjusted HR 3.24, 95 % CI 2.19–4.80; p < 0.001).

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.
pearl: Time‑below‑threshold and area‑under‑curve deficit of MAP during the first 24 h, particularly at thresholds of 65–80 mmHg, were independently associated with 90‑day mortality.

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial
author: Yokoyama R et al.

source_paper: Association between time to target mean arterial pressure and 90-day mortality in septic shock: a post hoc analysis of the OPTPRESS trial

author: Yokoyama R et al.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Ultra‑short‑acting beta‑blockers (esmolol, landiolol) should be used only in carefully selected patients with a maladaptive adrenergic phenotype and titrated under continuous hemodynamic monitoring; mortality benefit remains unproven.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Routine therapeutic‑dose systemic anticoagulation for sepsis‑associated new‑onset atrial fibrillation is not recommended during the acute unstable phase; decisions should be individualized and reassessed after ICU discharge.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Correct reversible triggers before or concurrently with antiarrhythmic therapy: target serum potassium ≥4.0 mmol/L, magnesium ≥1.0 mmol/L, minimize catecholamine exposure, and optimize fluid balance.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Sepsis‑induced diastolic dysfunction occurs in 40–60 % of patients but is not independently associated with 28‑day mortality.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Norepinephrine increases atrial automaticity; dopamine doses >5 µg/kg/min and dobutamine frequently precipitate tachyarrhythmias.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: In the hemodynamically unstable phenotype, immediate synchronized cardioversion is a Class I recommendation in 2024 ESC guidelines.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M
pearl: Left‑ventricular ejection fraction <35 % identifies patients where negative inotropic agents should be avoided; amiodarone or digoxin are preferred.

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: Treatment of supraventricular arrhythmias in critical care patients with sepsis
author: Tencer T, Waldauf P, Balik M

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Diagnose CDI with ≥3 unformed stools in 24 h plus supportive lab testing; multistep testing (PCR followed by toxin EIA) is preferred to distinguish infection from colonization.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Classify CDI severity at diagnosis: WBC > 15,000 cells/µL and/or creatinine > 1.5× baseline indicates severe disease; shock, ileus, or megacolon defines fulminant disease with 30–40% mortality.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 10 days is first‑line therapy for nonfulminant CDI because it lowers recurrence rates compared with vancomycin.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days is an acceptable alternative first‑line therapy for nonfulminant CDI.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Metronidazole should not be used outside of fulminant disease due to inferior efficacy and higher recurrence risk.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: A ≥50% reduction in loose stools should be seen within 3–5 days of therapy; if not, consider alternative diagnoses.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: For a first recurrence (within 8 weeks), treat with a vancomycin taper/pulse regimen or fidaxomicin EXTEND pulsed protocol.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Vancomycin taper/pulse regimen: 125 mg 4×/day for 14 days, then 125 mg 2×/day for 14 days, then 125 mg 1×/day for 14 days, then every third day for 14 days.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Fidaxomicin EXTEND pulsed protocol: 200 mg twice daily days 1–5, then once daily on alternate days days 7–25.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Offer fecal microbiota–based therapies after a second recurrence (third episode); consider earlier use in high‑risk patients or severe initial episodes.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Prior to FMT, complete a course of standard CDI antimicrobial treatment and maintain suppressive oral vancomycin ≥125 mg daily until therapy is available.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Stop antibacterial therapy 1–4 days before microbiota restoration.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Severely immunocompromised patients (e.g., cytotoxic chemotherapy, recent stem cell transplant, ANC < 500 cells/µL, CD4 < 200 mm³) should defer FMT in favor of antibiotic suppression.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Prolonged, low‑dose suppressive vancomycin (125 mg daily) may be considered for patients with multiple recurrent episodes who are not candidates for FMT.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Recurrent CDI risk exceeds 30% after discontinuation of vancomycin prophylaxis.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Systemic absorption of oral vancomycin is extremely limited; no detectable serum levels in 98% of patients even with renal insufficiency.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Treat fulminant CDI with vancomycin 500 mg orally/enterally every 6 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours; add rectal vancomycin 500 mg every 6 hours if ileus is present.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: After 3–5 days of clinical response, narrow to vancomycin 125 mg four times daily; treat for a minimum of 10 days, preferably 14 days.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Patients not responding or worsening after 48 hours of aggressive therapy should be considered for FMT or colectomy.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Routine test of cure should not be performed after CDI treatment; stool testing is reserved for persistent or worsening diarrheal symptoms.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Probiotics are not advised to prevent an initial or recurrent CDI.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Use of vancomycin during systemic antibiotic administration to prevent CDI is not advised.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: PPIs may increase CDI risk, but the absolute risk is low; if no indication, discuss cessation.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Patients with a history of CDI should be counseled to avoid unnecessary antibiotic therapy.

source_paper: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Management of Clostridioides difficile Infection in Adults: Expert Review
pearl: Cholestyramine and other bile‑acid binding agents should not be used as monotherapy or concurrently with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin.

source_paper: Prevention and treatment of thrombosis in patients with decompensated cirrhosis

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

source_paper: Guidelines for the management of emergencies and critical illness in pediatric and adult patients with sickle cell disease

pearl: Refer sickle cell patients with severe acute complications—such as ACS requiring respiratory support, acute anemia with DHTR risk, stroke, hepatic VOC, splenic sequestration, sepsis, or priapism—to a reference center.

pearl: The DISTALS trial demonstrated that the Tigertriever 13 adjustable stent retriever achieved a 3‑fold higher successful reperfusion rate without symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage (86.3% vs 27.7%, P<0.001) in codominant/nondominant M2 occlusions.

pearl: Computed tomography alone may underestimate PRES; MRI with FLAIR and DWI is essential for accurate diagnosis.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Neurological complications are common and underrecognized in critically ill patients without primary brain injury; autopsy studies show acute brain injury in nearly half of patients.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Delirium occurs in about one-third of ICU patients and independently predicts higher mortality, longer mechanical ventilation, and long‑term cognitive impairment comparable to moderate traumatic brain injury or mild Alzheimer’s disease.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Over 90% of seizures in critically ill patients are non‑convulsive; continuous electroencephalography is required for detection.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: ICU‑acquired weakness occurs in more than 40% of ICU patients and is linked to prolonged ventilation, increased 5‑year mortality, and persistent functional impairment; risk factors include sepsis, hyperglycemia, corticosteroids, and neuromuscular blockers.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: 30–50% of ICU survivors have long‑term cognitive impairment at 6–12 months; prolonged delirium duration is a major predictor.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Deep or prolonged benzodiazepine sedation, immobility, sleep disruption, and metabolic derangements are modifiable risk factors for delirium; the ABCDEF bundle reduces delirium and improves outcomes.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Cefepime neurotoxicity occurs in up to 15% of ICU patients and carries a ten‑fold higher risk of delirium/neurotoxicity than meropenem.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Clinical detection of stroke in ICU patients is only 5%, whereas autopsy shows brain injury in nearly 50%.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Hypoactive delirium accounts for 40–60% of ICU delirium presentations and is often missed without structured screening.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: One‑year post‑ICU cognitive impairment prevalence (~34%) is comparable to moderate traumatic brain injury and mild Alzheimer’s disease.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: 8–15% of ICU patients experience seizures; >90% are non‑convulsive; continuous EEG detects more but has not improved outcomes.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: CT/CTA is the primary diagnostic modality for stroke in ICU; many patients have contraindications to thrombolysis.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: ICU‑acquired weakness is diagnosed when the Medical Research Council sum score is <48 or handgrip strength is <11 kg (male) / <7 kg (female).

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: ECMO is associated with 7% neurologic complications; a large drop in PaCO₂ within 24 h predicts higher risk.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Continuous renal replacement therapy provides slower solute removal and may reduce cerebral edema risk compared to intermittent hemodialysis.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: The ABCDEF bundle (pain assessment, awakening trials, sedation choice, delirium monitoring, early mobility, family engagement) improves delirium and outcomes.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Continuous EEG in high‑risk ICU patients with unexplained coma or fluctuating mental status can improve detection of non‑convulsive status epilepticus.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Dexmedetomidine is the preferred sedative in ICU to reduce delirium prevalence.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Benzodiazepine sedation increases delirium incidence and duration compared with alternative agents.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Beta‑lactam antibiotics (cefepime, imipenem) are strongly associated with encephalopathy and seizures.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Fluoroquinolones frequently cause agitation, insomnia, delirium, and can trigger seizures.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Linezolid can cause serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with other serotonergic drugs.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) can cause drug‑induced encephalopathy and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: High PEEP during mechanical ventilation can raise intracranial pressure; hyperventilation‑induced hypocapnia causes cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia risk.

source_paper: Burden of and risk factors for neurological complications in critical illness
author: Victoria A. McCredie et al.
pearl: Early structured mobility programs in ICU reduce delirium and improve neurocognitive outcomes.
