pearl: Polymyxin‑based regimens are associated with a twofold higher odds of adverse events and a trend toward increased mortality compared with newer agents, supporting a shift away from polymyxins in critically ill patients.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Isavuconazole is recommended as first‑line therapy for ICU invasive pulmonary aspergillosis, with evidence strength equivalent to voriconazole (A I/II); it is preferred in critically ill patients because of better pharmacokinetics and a lower risk of neuro‑ and hepatotoxicity and no QTc prolongation.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Serial serum galactomannan screening twice weekly is recommended for high‑risk hematologic ICU patients (A I); for other high‑risk groups such as severe influenza, screening is a marginal recommendation (C II).

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Bronchoscopy with BALF testing (direct microscopy, culture, GM, Aspergillus PCR) is strongly recommended (A II) for any ICU patient with new radiological infiltrates without an alternative explanation or a positive non‑invasive Aspergillus test.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: In non‑immunocompromised ICU patients, probable IPA requires host criteria (severe influenza, COVID‑19, COPD, liver disease) plus a new pulmonary infiltrate and a positive BALF GM with a cut‑off ≥1 ODI, culture, or two positive serum PCRs.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended for voriconazole (target trough 1.5–5 mg/L) and posaconazole (target trough >1 mg/L for therapy) starting at day 5, but is not routinely required for isavuconazole.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: For suspected or documented azole resistance, liposomal amphotericin B is the first‑line agent (A II); combination therapy with a triazole and an echinocandin may be considered for severe cases (C I/III).

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Posaconazole prophylaxis is recommended for high‑risk hematologic patients (A I) but not for severe influenza (D I); it may be considered in severe COVID‑19 if local CAPA incidence is ≥10 % (C II).

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: IPA incidence in the general ICU population is <1 %; risk stratification defines low risk 1–5 %, intermediate 5–10 %, and high >10 %.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: High‑risk conditions include hematologic cancer with prolonged neutropenia (≥10 days) not on mould‑active prophylaxis, allogeneic HCT without prophylaxis, and severe influenza.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Intermediate‑risk conditions include lung/heart transplant, severe liver disease (Child‑Pugh C, MELD >24), severe COPD (GOLD 3/4), and severe COVID‑19.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Pooled incidence of IAPA in critically ill patients is 20–30 %; CAPA incidence varies 0–47 % with a pooled rate of 10 %.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Chest CT is recommended for any patient with worsening respiratory conditions (A III); typical IPA findings appear in ≤25 % of cases, while non‑specific consolidation/ground‑glass opacities are seen in >70 %.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Posaconazole is dosed 300 mg q12h on day 1, then 300 mg q24h; increase to 400 mg q24h if weight >140 kg.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: Isavuconazole is dosed IV/Oral 200 mg q8h on days 1–2 then 200 mg q24h.

source_paper: Management of Invasive Pulmonary Aspergillosis in Intensive Care Units: Guidelines From the Fungal Infection Network of Switzerland (FUNGINOS)
pearl: In severe liver dysfunction, isavuconazole or liposomal amphotericin B should be favored over voriconazole.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use an aseptic "no‑touch" technique for PIVC insertion; use a sterile technique (mask, gown, gloves, drape) for PICC and PAC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Apply 2% chlorhexidine gluconate in 70% alcohol for skin disinfection; ensure chlorhexidine concentration >0.5%, and avoid >2% in neonates <36 weeks.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: All clinicians inserting peripheral catheters must complete a formal training programme with demonstrated competency; this is essential for PICC/PAC and for all catheters in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Inspect PIVCs at least daily for inflammation or infection; consider removal within 72–96 h or when clinically indicated.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Prefer single‑lumen PICCs over multi‑lumen PICCs unless multiple lumens are clinically required, to reduce infection and thrombosis risk.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Perform hand hygiene at all five moments during catheter insertion, maintenance, access, and removal, using WHO alcohol‑based hand rub or soap and water.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Wear single‑use gloves when inserting a PIVC or PICC in adults, adolescents, or children; use sterile gloves for PICC/PAC; do not use gloves for PIVC if aseptic "no‑touch" technique is strictly followed.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use a standardized insertion pack or kit for PICC or PAC insertion; for PIVC use a standardized pack/kit as well.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use ultrasound guidance when inserting a PICC; routine ultrasound is not recommended for PIVC insertion.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Select distal arm veins over proximal (cubital fossa) for PIVC insertion; choose upper limb over lower limb for PIVC and PICC in neonates; avoid scalp veins in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Apply either an occlusive or non‑occlusive dressing for PIVCs; use an occlusive dressing for PICCs.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Maintain all PIVCs, PICCs, and PACs with a formal sterile dressing protocol.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Flush the catheter with a compatible sterile fluid (saline or other) after each product administration.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Prefer sterile saline for lock‑off flushing over heparinised saline to avoid heparin‑induced thrombocytopenia.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Change administration sets (tubing) on a regular schedule for PIVCs and PICCs.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Access catheters using a formal sterile or aseptic protocol.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use closed‑access hub systems for PIVCs and PICCs to reduce CRBSI in PICCs and sepsis in neonates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: If a PIVC is inserted under uncontrolled or emergency conditions, remove or replace it as soon as possible, ideally within 24 h.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Scheduled removal of PIVCs can be performed at 72–96 h; clinically indicated removal is also acceptable.

source_paper: Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters. Part I: peripheral catheters
pearl: Use a multi‑lumen catheter for IV access only if the expected duration is less than 14 days; otherwise, choose a PICC.

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Give the first empiric antibiotic dose within 1 hour of presentation; do not delay for lumbar puncture or imaging.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: The classic triad of fever (≥38 °C), neck stiffness, and altered mental status is present in <50 % of bacterial meningitis cases; absence of all three lowers the likelihood of a bacterial cause.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Obtain CSF and blood for culture and PCR as soon as possible, ideally before starting antimicrobials, to maximize pathogen detection.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Empiric therapy for adults and children >1 month: IV ceftriaxone 2 g q12 h (or cefotaxime); add ampicillin or amoxicillin if Listeria risk factors (age > 60, pregnancy, immunocompromised).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Adjunctive IV dexamethasone 10 mg q6 h (0.15 mg/kg in children) should start with the first antibiotic dose when bacterial meningitis is strongly suspected; discontinue if any pathogen other than S. pneumoniae or H. influenzae b is identified.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In HIV‑associated cryptococcal meningitis, therapeutic lumbar puncture to reduce CSF opening pressure to <20 cm H₂O improves clinical outcome and survival, regardless of initial pressure.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: All patients who survive acute meningitis should have a complete neurological exam and formal audiological screening for hearing loss before discharge.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Use the Interagency Integrated Triage Tool (IITT) to identify high‑risk patients with abnormal vital signs for immediate review.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Absolute contraindications to lumbar puncture include ongoing hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, severe bleeding disorder, skin infection over the puncture site, or epidural abscess near the site.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Relative contraindications requiring cranial imaging before lumbar puncture are GCS < 10, focal neurological signs, cranial nerve deficits, papilledema, new‑onset seizures in adults, or severe immunocompromise.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Collect at least three CSF tubes (≥1 mL each) for cellularity, biochemistry, Gram stain, culture, and PCR; if only one tube is available, prioritize microbiology.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Core CSF investigations: glucose, total protein, WBC count (total and differential), RBC count, lactate (if antibiotics not yet started), Gram stain, culture, AST, and PCR for bacteria and viruses.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: For suspected TB meningitis, use LC‑aNAAT (e.g., Xpert MTB/RIF Ultra); for suspected cryptococcal meningitis in advanced HIV, perform rapid CSF CrAg assay (or India ink if CrAg unavailable).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Cloudy CSF with neutrophil predominance, low glucose, and high protein suggests bacterial meningitis; clear CSF with lymphocyte predominance and normal/moderately altered glucose and protein suggests viral meningitis.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In malaria‑endemic areas, perform a parasitological test; if positive, treat for severe malaria but still perform lumbar puncture to rule out bacterial meningitis.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Empiric therapy for neonates: ampicillin plus gentamicin, or cefotaxime plus gentamicin, or ceftriaxone plus gentamicin.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Adjunctive dexamethasone (10 mg q6 h, 0.15 mg/kg in children) should be started with the first antibiotic dose within 12 h; continue up to 4 days if S. pneumoniae or H. influenzae b is identified; discontinue if other pathogens are found; do not give if cerebral malaria or cryptococcal meningitis is suspected.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Definitive antibiotic durations: S. pneumoniae 10–14 days; N. meningitidis 5–7 days; H. influenzae b 7–10 days; S. agalactiae 14–21 days; L. monocytogenes 21 days; Gram‑negative bacilli 14–21 days.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: If the pathogen remains unidentified, discontinue empiric antibiotics after 7 days if the patient is afebrile, vitals stable, and consciousness normal for ≥48 hours.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: TB meningitis treatment: adults – intensive 2 months HRZE, continuation 7–10 months HR (total 9–12 months); children – standard 12 months (2HRZE/10HR) or short 6‑month intensive regimen (6HRZEto) if no DR‑TB risk factors or HIV.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Cryptococcal meningitis induction: liposomal amphotericin B IV single high dose 10 mg/kg (or 3–4 mg/kg/day) plus flucytosine PO 25 mg/kg q6h and fluconazole PO/IV 1200 mg/day for 14 days, followed by consolidation and maintenance fluconazole.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Increased intracranial pressure ≥20 cm H₂O in adults is life‑threatening; signs include severe headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, and Cushing reflex.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Basic emergency measures for raised ICP: secure airway, avoid hypotension, maintain SpO₂ > 94 %, control fever <37.5 °C, elevate head 15–30°, consider hyperventilation if ventilated (target PaCO₂ 26–30 mmHg).
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: In cryptococcal meningitis, therapeutic lumbar puncture to reduce CSF pressure to <20 cm H₂O (or halve baseline) is recommended; mannitol, acetazolamide, furosemide, and corticosteroids are not recommended.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis, treatment and care
pearl: Seizures occur in 20–30 % of meningitis patients; acute symptomatic seizures are treated with IV benzodiazepines followed by second‑line agents if seizures persist.
file_name: WHO practical manual on meningitis diagnosis.json

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Critical care is a treatment, not a location; intensivists should support care outside the ICU.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Pregnant patients are unfasted and at high aspiration risk; use rapid sequence induction with cricoid pressure and video laryngoscopy as first choice.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Maintain maternal SpO2 >95% and PaO2 >9.3 kPa (70 mmHg) to ensure adequate fetal oxygenation.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Noradrenaline is the preferred first‑line vasopressor in obstetric shock; vasopressin should be avoided.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Initiate renal replacement therapy when serum urea reaches 17–20 mmol/L in pregnancy.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: During major obstetric haemorrhage resuscitation, keep fibrinogen >2 g/L using fibrinogen concentrate or cryoprecipitate.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: In pre‑eclampsia, give high‑dose remifentanil during intubation to blunt the hypertensive response and restrict fluids to 80 mL hr⁻¹.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Prone positioning is safe for pregnant patients with severe hypoxaemia when continuous fetal monitoring is used.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: In shock, rule out inferior vena cava compression and use a 15–30° lateral tilt or left lateral decubitus position.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Caution with fluid boluses; 30 mL kg⁻¹ may cause significant oedema in pregnancy.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Ephedrine worsens fetal acidaemia in a dose‑dependent manner.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Remifentanil is the first‑line opioid for sedation in obstetric ICU due to reliable neonatal metabolism.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Avoid NSAIDs after 28 weeks gestation because of risk of premature ductus arteriosus closure.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Target a blood glucose level of 6–8 mmol L⁻¹ in critically ill pregnant patients.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Iodinated contrast is safe in pregnancy; gadolinium contrast should be avoided.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Tranexamic acid is recommended for all cases of postpartum haemorrhage.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Early delivery is recommended for obstetric syndromes such as pre‑eclampsia and HELLP; timing for non‑obstetric critical illness should be individualized by an MDT.

source_paper: Maternal Critical Care: A Narrative Review
author: Alex Warrilow-Wilson et al.
pearl: Risk of major bleeding with thrombolysis for pulmonary embolism is higher postpartum (58 %) than antepartum (18 %).

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Patients with CRP >100 mg/dL or IL‑6 >11.17 pg/mL may not gain the same survival benefit from full nutritional therapy and require a more individualized approach.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Set energy targets using indirect calorimetry and aim to reach them by day 5–7; predictive equations are unreliable in the acute phase.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Avoid protein >1.2 g/kg/day early in ICU; target 1.0–1.2 g/kg/day and increase gradually over the first 4–5 days.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: A high urea‑to‑creatinine ratio identifies a metabolically distinct ICU subgroup with increased protein catabolism and may predict reduced benefit from high protein intake.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Give thiamine 100 mg/day from admission and standard parenteral nutrition micronutrient doses for ~5 days; no blood monitoring is needed.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: A 4:1 ketogenic diet with β‑hydroxybutyrate 1–3 mmol/L is feasible in refractory status epilepticus and sepsis, reducing vasopressor use and improving glucose control.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Post‑ICU survivors typically meet only 50–70 % of energy and protein needs; structured multidisciplinary rehabilitation and transitional nutrition programs are essential.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Interpret micronutrient blood levels together with CRP because inflammation redistributes micronutrients.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Treat macronutrients as pharmacotherapy; personalize to metabolic tolerance and organ function.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Order nutrition as a rate (1 ml/kg/h ≈ 24 kcal/kg/day) to simplify monitoring and adjustment.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Early initiation of a 4:1 ketogenic diet with BHB 1–3 mmol/L improves seizure resolution in adult super‑refractory status epilepticus.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Ketogenic nutrition in sepsis improves glucose homeostasis, eliminates insulin need, and increases ventilator‑free, vasopressor‑free, dialysis‑free, and ICU‑free days.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: Repeat BIA and handgrip strength every 4–6 weeks for the first 6 months, then every 3 months, in post‑ICU patients.

source_paper: Current concepts on feeding the critically ill patient: a narrative review
author: Michael Adolph et al.
pearl: The EFFORT II trial evaluates a multidisciplinary outpatient nutrition program to improve long‑term survival and functional recovery after ICU discharge.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An oxygen extraction ratio (O2ER) >30% indicates that oxygen delivery (DO₂) is insufficient to meet metabolic demands.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, septic shock, resuscitation

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER <20% may reflect impaired cellular oxygen utilization, shunting, or excessive oxygen delivery.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, septic shock, pathophysiology

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Normal O2ER values lie between 20% and 30% and represent adequate balance of supply and demand.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Targeting an O2ER of 20–30% during resuscitation can guide fluid therapy and avoid under‑ or over‑resuscitation.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER >40% signals inadequate DO₂ and should prompt optimization of preload, hemoglobin, cardiac output, and arterial oxygenation.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: An O2ER <20% combined with elevated ScvO₂ and rising lactate suggests impaired extraction; further increasing DO₂ alone is unlikely to help.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Changes in central venous oxygen saturation (ΔScvO₂) after fluid challenge identify fluid responders and correlate with cardiac index changes.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Lactate‑clearance‑guided resuscitation reduces in‑hospital mortality, shortens mechanical ventilation, and lowers severity scores compared with ScvO₂‑guided protocols.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Dynamic lactate measurements and lactate‑clearance trajectories predict 28‑day mortality better than static lactate levels.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Combining lactate and ScvO₂ levels stratifies mortality more effectively; elevated lactate with normal ScvO₂ is a strong predictor of poor outcome.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: The ΔPv‑aCO₂/CaO₂‑CvO₂ ratio can detect early perfusion deficits and monitor metabolic shifts during resuscitation.
topic: CO₂ gap, perfusion monitoring, septic shock

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: ScvO₂ normalization alone is no longer a sufficient universal therapeutic target in contemporary septic shock phenotypes.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign removed ScvO₂ ≥70% as a mandated target after neutral trials showed no mortality benefit.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: ScvO₂ alone is insufficient to characterize circulatory status; multimodal integration with metabolic and perfusion indices is required.

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: Dynamic changes in O2ER often precede overt deterioration in vital signs, enabling earlier detection of occult hypoperfusion.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, early detection, septic shock

source_paper: Central venous oxygen saturation vs. oxygen extraction ratio in septic shock resuscitation: Which metric is more informative?
author: Chalkias A, et al.
pearl: O2ER accuracy depends on reliable measurement of arterial and venous oxygen content and can be distorted by supraphysiologic DO₂ or microcirculatory shunting.
topic: Oxygen extraction ratio, measurement accuracy, septic shock

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Sarcopenia affects 40–70% of sepsis survivors and can persist up to 2 years after ICU discharge, significantly impacting long‑term quality of life and mortality.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: A 1% reduction in rectus femoris cross‑sectional area on bedside ultrasound is associated with a 6% increase in 28‑day mortality, making it an independent prognostic factor.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Diagnosis of sepsis‑associated sarcopenia relies on non‑invasive measures such as handgrip strength, MRC score, bedside ultrasound of rectus femoris or diaphragm, and CT‑derived muscle indices (PMI, SMI); muscle biopsy is not routinely recommended.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Initiating enteral nutrition within 48 hours of hemodynamic stabilization is associated with lower in‑hospital mortality in patients with pre‑existing sarcopenia (OCEBM Level 2b).

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Early mobilization within the first 3 days of ICU admission significantly improves outcomes (OCEBM Level 2b).

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Ultrasound assessment of diaphragm and intercostal muscle echogenicity and thickening fraction may help predict weaning outcomes.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Reduced psoas muscle index on CT independently correlates with 28‑ and 90‑day mortality and longer ICU/hospital stay.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Low skeletal muscle index on CT is associated with higher in‑hospital mortality, multiple organ failure, and 1‑year mortality.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: Neuromuscular electrical stimulation is safe and may enhance muscle strength and compound muscle action potentials, though large‑scale efficacy trials are lacking.

source_paper: Sepsis-associated sarcopenia: Emerging insights into mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
author: Xiujun Chang et al.
pearl: No specific clinical guidelines exist for sepsis‑associated sarcopenia; management is extrapolated from general critical‑care nutrition and rehabilitation principles, focusing on preventing net protein catabolism.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Fever of unknown origin is defined as a temperature ≥38.3 °C (101 °F) lasting >3 weeks that remains undiagnosed after a standard work‑up, and it is now often evaluated as an outpatient.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The diagnostic work‑up for FUO should be clue‑driven, first categorizing the case into malignant/neoplastic, infectious, rheumatic/inflammatory, or miscellaneous disorders based on focused history and physical examination.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Early anorexia with significant weight loss (>2 lb/week) strongly suggests a malignant or neoplastic etiology.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Chills are common in infectious FUOs, whereas rigors essentially rule out rheumatic or inflammatory causes.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: An erythrocyte sedimentation rate >100 mm/h markedly narrows the differential to a few entities.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The proportion of atypical lymphocytes matters: 6 % atypical lymphocytes point to drug fever or toxoplasmosis, while 36 % atypical lymphocytes suggest EBV or CMV.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: The Naprosyn test (375 mg naproxen twice daily for 3 days) is positive when the temperature falls markedly over 3 days, indicating a malignant or neoplastic FUO; a negative test (persistent fever) suggests an infectious etiology.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Posterior cervical, supra‑/infraclavicular, or epitrochlear lymph node biopsies have higher diagnostic yield than anterior cervical, axillary, or inguinal node biopsies in FUO.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: In HIV‑associated FUO, drug fever occurs in 3–20 % of cases, and immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) typically develops within 60 days of initiating HAART.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Routine blood cultures are unnecessary for non‑bacteremic FUOs and should be avoided early in the work‑up.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: An isolated alkaline phosphatase elevation is suggestive of lymphoma in the FUO work‑up.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Monoclonal gammopathy on serum protein electrophoresis points to multiple myeloma, hyper‑IgD syndrome, or multicentric Castleman disease.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Microscopic hematuria may be the sole clue to subacute bacterial endocarditis, renal tuberculosis, brucellosis, PAN, lymphoma, or renal cell carcinoma.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: PET‑CT is most useful for obscure infectious or neoplastic FUOs, such as lymphomas, Erdheim‑Chester disease, Q fever endocarditis, or aortic graft infection.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Bone marrow biopsy can be diagnostic for many FUO causes and may be positive when blood cultures are negative in subacute bacterial endocarditis or typhoid fever.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Empiric therapy should be reserved for life‑threatening FUOs such as CNS or miliary tuberculosis or giant cell arteritis/temporal arteritis.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Recurrent FUO is defined as at least two episodes of prolonged fever separated by ≥2 weeks of fever‑free intervals; if it remains undiagnosed for >1 year, a definitive diagnosis is unlikely.

source_paper: Fever of Unknown Origin: A Clinical Approach
author: Burke A. Cunha, MD, Olivier Lortholary, MD, PhD, Cheston B. Cunha, MD
pearl: Antipyretic or antimicrobial therapy may mask, delay, or obscure clinical manifestations in FUO and should be avoided until a diagnosis is established.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: HFNC is strongly recommended over standard oxygen for adults with de novo acute hypoxemic respiratory failure to lower intubation rates (moderate certainty).

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: NIV via facemask is strongly recommended over standard oxygen for adults with acute hypercapnic respiratory failure to reduce mortality and intubation (moderate certainty).

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: HFNC can be used as a conditional alternative to NIV in hypercapnic respiratory failure with mild acidemia (pH > 7.25) if close monitoring and prompt escalation to NIV are available.

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: HFNC or NIV via facemask is strongly recommended over standard oxygen for preoxygenation before endotracheal intubation to prevent peri‑intubation hypoxemia (moderate certainty).

source_paper: Noninvasive Respiratory Support for Adult Patients with Acute Respiratory Failure: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline
pearl: Helmet NIV probably reduces intubation in hypoxemic failure (RR 0.22; moderate certainty).

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review

source_paper: Nonoperative Treatment of Appendicitis and Implications for Emergency Department Management: A Narrative Review

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Low‑dose aspirin 100–325 mg initiated within 6 hours after CABG and continued indefinitely improves graft patency and reduces cardiovascular events.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: In post‑CABG patients with ACS, dual antiplatelet therapy with ticagrelor or clopidogrel for 1 year reduces major adverse cardiovascular events; in stable CAD, DAPT may be considered for 1 year to prevent graft failure in low‑bleeding‑risk patients.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Treat LDL‑C to <55 mg/dL (1.4 mmol/L) as ideal and no higher than <70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L); use a high‑intensity statin first, then ezetimibe, then a PCSK9 inhibitor.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP‑1 receptor agonists are prioritized in post‑CABG patients with diabetes (regardless of HbA1c) and in those with heart failure regardless of diabetes or ejection fraction for major adverse cardiovascular event reduction.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Smoking cessation after CABG lowers 5‑year mortality by 35 % and major adverse cardiovascular events by 18 %; quitting even 4 weeks preoperatively yields outcomes comparable to never smokers.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: All post‑CABG patients should be referred to cardiac rehabilitation within 4 weeks of discharge; telerehabilitation is a noninferior alternative to center‑based programs.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: In patients with prior myocardial infarction and persistent left ventricular ejection fraction <40 %, simultaneous initiation of all four guideline‑directed medical therapy pillars (ARNi/ACE inhibitor, beta‑blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, SGLT2 inhibitor) is favored, supported by team‑based strategies.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Perioperative beta‑blockers are used to prevent atrial fibrillation in high‑risk patients; continuation in those already on beta‑blockers is associated with lower 30‑day mortality only in patients with LVEF ≤40 %.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: ACE inhibitors or angiotensin‑receptor blockers are indicated for patients who have a clinical indication other than CABG (prior myocardial infarction, diabetes, heart failure, reduced ejection fraction, or hypertension).

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Calcium‑channel blockers, preferably dihydropyridines such as amlodipine or nifedipine, for the first postoperative year may help limit radial artery graft spasm.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Preoperative hemoglobin A1c <6.5 % correlates with fewer perioperative complications; target <7 % when applicable.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: SGLT2 inhibitors should be withheld 3 days before CABG and restarted once oral intake resumes postoperatively.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: GLP‑1 receptor agonists should be stopped 1 week before surgery (once‑weekly agents) or 3 days before (oral agents) and resumed when bowel motility is restored.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Influenza and pneumococcal vaccines should be considered in individuals with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Assess psychosocial risk factors and mood disturbance through clinical interviews or standardized tools before and after surgery.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: When a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor is initiated in patients with coronary artery disease on antiplatelet therapy, gastroprotective agents are considered.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Cardiac prehabilitation and cardiac rehabilitation programs enhance physical health and alleviate symptoms of depression, anxiety, and hostility.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Cardiac rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance, reduces hospitalizations, major adverse cardiovascular events, and mortality.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Cardiac rehabilitation may mitigate postoperative cognitive impairment.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Early mobilization based on deep‑breathing exercises and chest physiotherapy reduces postoperative cognitive impairment and pain.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Weight management primary goals are to prevent further weight gain, achieve weight reduction, and sustain long‑term weight loss.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Pharmacotherapy (GLP‑1 receptor agonists, tirzepatide, orlistat, phentermine/topiramate, naltrexone/bupropion) is recommended for BMI ≥30 kg/m² and BMI >27 kg/m² with weight‑related comorbidities who have failed lifestyle interventions.

source_paper: Secondary Prevention After Coronary Artery Bypass Graft Surgery: 2026 Update: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
pearl: Semaglutide versus placebo shows significant and consistent major adverse cardiovascular event reductions; numerically greater absolute risk reduction (2.3 % vs 1.0 %) at 3 years in patients with a history of CABG.

author: Aristi Boulmpou et al.

author: Sobolewska J, Dzialach L, Kuca P, Witek P

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Dispatch ALS units when any of the following are present on the phone call: respiratory rate >25 breaths/min, thoraco‑abdominal asynchrony, inability to count to 20 in one breath (Roth score), peripheral cyanosis, profuse sweating, or GCS <15.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Direct ICU admission (bypass ED) is recommended for patients receiving prehospital invasive mechanical ventilation, non‑invasive ventilation, high‑flow nasal oxygen, or who have at least one additional organ failure such as shock or altered consciousness.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: A respiratory rate >25 breaths/min is strongly linked to higher rates of endotracheal intubation, ICU admission, and in‑hospital mortality.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Altered consciousness, paradoxical abdominal breathing, and accessory muscle use are strong predictors of in‑hospital mortality in respiratory distress.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Triage patients with respiratory distress using a validated structured scale (e.g., FRENCH, Manchester, ESI, CTAS) to prioritize high‑risk cases.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: High‑risk patients (Triage 1/2) require continuous monitoring of SBP, HR, RR, temperature, GCS, and SpO2; low‑risk patients (Triage 3) need regular intermittent monitoring of the same parameters.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Measure respiratory rate over a minimum of 30 seconds upon arrival and continuously thereafter.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Use the Modified Borg Scale (0–10) to quantify subjective dyspnea intensity.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: For patients with very low clinical suspicion of pulmonary embolism, apply the PERC rule; it has a false‑negative rate below 1% and can safely exclude PE without further testing.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: The YEARS or PEGeD algorithms are preferable to standard D‑dimer testing with Geneva/Wells scores; they reduce CTPA use by 14–17.6% while maintaining low failure rates.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Strict adherence to the chosen PE diagnostic algorithm reduces the risk of venous thromboembolism by 4.29‑fold.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: VBG is not recommended for assessing hypoxemia; a SpO₂ >96% (or >92% in COPD) makes PaO₂ <60 mmHg highly unlikely.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: A venous PvCO₂ <45 mmHg rules out arterial hypercapnia >50 mmHg; an elevated PvCO₂ cannot reliably quantify arterial hypercapnia.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: ABG should be reserved for confirming ventilatory support, guiding disposition, unreliable SpO₂, or quantifying hypercapnia/acidosis—not performed systematically.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: BNP <100 pg/mL or NT‑proBNP <300–500 pg/mL effectively rules out acute heart failure with a negative predictive value near 90%; interpret with renal function and BMI.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Integrated cardiac and lung ultrasound is recommended for dyspnea evaluation; it improves diagnostic accuracy by 24%.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: Low‑dose chest CT is suggested when there is diagnostic uncertainty in community‑acquired pneumonia or when a standard chest X‑ray is inadequate.

source_paper: Guidelines for the Initial Assessment of Respiratory Distress in the Emergency Department
pearl: In patients aged 65 or older, assess frailty with the Clinical Frailty Scale, Katz ADL, or Barthel Index to predict in‑hospital survival; chronological age alone is a poor predictor.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Stop any suspected causative drug immediately; essential, unsuspected drugs should be continued to avoid compromising critical therapy.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Sepsis is the leading cause of death in EN; perform strict asepsis for all invasive procedures and obtain superficial skin cultures twice weekly until healing; prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Use the modified Brooke formula for initial fluid resuscitation: V24h (mL) = 1.5 × BSA% × body weight (kg); adjust fluids to maintain urine output of 0.5–1 mL/kg/h, reassessing every 4–8 h.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Early intubation should be considered in patients with signs of tracheobronchial injury or upper airway obstruction; non‑invasive ventilation is not recommended in EN.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Transfer to a high‑volume, multidisciplinary reference center when detached/detachable BSA exceeds 10 % or organ failure (shock, anuria, acute respiratory failure) is present; such centers independently reduce in‑hospital mortality.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Long‑term sequelae affect up to 90 % of EN survivors; multidisciplinary follow‑up for at least one year is essential to address ocular, psychological, and pigmentary complications.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Define SJS as <10 % BSA detachment, TEN as ≥30 % BSA, and overlap as 10–29 % BSA; these thresholds guide severity assessment and management decisions.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: EN typically occurs 4–28 days after drug exposure; high‑risk agents include aromatic antiepileptics, allopurinol, sulfonamides, oxicams, and nevirapine.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Mortality averages 15–20 %; SCORTEN and ABCD‑10 scores predict risk, with older age, CKD, pneumonia, sepsis, and malignancy increasing mortality.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Screen for HLA‑B*15:02 before initiating carbamazepine and HLA‑B*58:01 before allopurinol to reduce EN risk in susceptible populations.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Avoid topical antibiotics (e.g., silver sulfadiazine) and topical corticosteroids; they increase sensitization and antimicrobial resistance without benefit.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: In septic shock with Candida skin colonization, consider preemptive antifungal therapy; skin involvement near 100 % BSA is a major risk factor for candidemia.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Use antibiotic‑ or antiseptic‑coated central venous catheters (e.g., chlorhexidine‑glucose/silver sulfadiazine) to halve catheter‑related bloodstream infection rates in EN patients.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Perform bedside ophthalmologic evaluation within 24 h; protect cornea with preservative‑free eye drops or vitamin A ointment every 2–4 h (hourly if severe) to prevent ocular sequelae.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Intubated EN patients should undergo bronchoscopy to identify tracheobronchial injuries; bronchoscopic suction reduces atelectasis from mucus plugs.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Maintain room temperature between 28–32 °C to create a thermoneutral environment and prevent hypothermia in EN patients.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Early renal replacement therapy is indicated if the offending drug is renally cleared and the patient develops acute kidney injury; RRT is required in 9 % of cases and is associated with 80 % mortality.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Immediately discontinue any suspected causative drug in EN; essential medications unrelated to the reaction should be continued to avoid iatrogenic harm.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Do not use prophylactic antibiotics in EN; treat bloodstream infections only when culture‑proven sepsis occurs, as prophylaxis increases resistance without mortality benefit.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Use the modified Brooke formula for initial fluid resuscitation: V24h = 1.5 × BSA (%) × body weight (kg); adjust to maintain urine output 0.5–1 mL/kg/h.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Non‑invasive ventilation is contraindicated in TEN due to facial lesions and aspiration risk; anticipate early orotracheal intubation if any tracheobronchial injury is suspected.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Transfer patients with >10% BSA detachment or any organ failure to a high‑volume, multidisciplinary EN center; this reduces in‑hospital mortality by up to 30%.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Topical antibiotics (e.g., silver sulfadiazine) and corticosteroids are not recommended; instead, cleanse with chlorhexidine and dress with non‑adherent materials or petroleum jelly.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Perform bedside ophthalmology within 24 h; give preservative‑free artificial tears and vitamin A ointment q2–4 h; consider amniotic membrane transplantation for severe ocular involvement.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Screen for bloodstream infection twice weekly with skin swabs; a negative culture has high negative predictive value for Staph aureus and Pseudomonas; treat sepsis guided by culture data.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Maintain ICU room temperature between 28–32 °C to reduce heat loss and prevent hypothermia in patients with extensive skin loss.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: SCORTEN and ABCD‑10 scores predict mortality; a SCORTEN ≥3 or ABCD‑10 ≥4 identifies patients at >50% risk of death.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.

source_paper: Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis: management in the ICU
author: Camille Windsor et al.
pearl: Long‑term follow‑up is essential; up to 90% of EN survivors develop pigmentary changes, ocular sequelae, or PTSD, necessitating multidisciplinary rehabilitation.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: In ICU patients, usual-care mobilisation should be light to moderate intensity, targeting a Borg RPE of 11–13 and a heart‑rate increase of ≤20% from resting; maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg without vasopressor escalation to avoid adverse events.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Structured safety screening before each session must flag absolute contraindications (haemodynamic collapse, uncontrolled arrhythmia, uncontrolled ICP, active bleeding) and relative contraindications (high-dose vasopressors, high ventilatory support, delirium, recent high‑dislodgement risk procedures).

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Apply a traffic‑light checklist: proceed if FiO₂ ≤ 0.6, PEEP ≤ 10–15 cmH₂O, and MAP ≥ 65 mmHg; otherwise modify or defer mobilisation.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Progression should be adaptive: after each session assess tolerance and either maintain, progress, or regress the activity level; avoid a fixed escalation sequence.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Physiotherapists managing higher‑risk patients must demonstrate competency in ICU mobilisation, including ventilator/line management and adverse event recognition.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: For patients limited to in‑bed activity, limit sessions to ≤10 minutes; additional time does not improve outcomes and may increase risk.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Use the lowest mobility level that achieves therapeutic intent; unnecessary escalation increases risk without added benefit.

source_paper: Practical Guide: Usual-care mobilisation of adult patients in the intensive care unit
author: Paton M, Hodgson CL
pearl: Document each session’s mobility level, active time, patient‑reported exertion/dyspnea, tolerance, and adverse events; reassess at every session to guide progression.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: ICU CDI incidence is 4.93 cases per 1,000 ICU admissions, more than double the overall rate (2.56/1,000). Severe ICU CDI carries a mortality of ~40%.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Fidaxomicin is the first‑line agent for initial and recurrent CDI in the ICU, offering lower recurrence rates and minimal microbiota disruption compared to vancomycin; oral vancomycin remains acceptable when cost limits fidaxomicin use.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Bezlotoxumab, the only FDA‑approved anti‑toxin monoclonal antibody for CDI recurrence prevention, was withdrawn in 2025, leaving no approved biologic to reduce recurrence.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: In elderly ICU patients, classic severity markers such as fever and leukocytosis are blunted by immunosenescence, rendering ATLAS and similar scores unreliable; clinicians should rely on clinical judgment and alternative markers.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Post‑operative ICU patients are high‑risk: colorectal surgery patients have a 1.5% CDI incidence, cardiac surgery 1.1% with 21.4% mortality, and vascular lower‑extremity amputation 2.6% incidence.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Male ICU patients with CDI have a 17% higher adjusted odds of in‑hospital mortality and longer stays, whereas female patients have a 12% higher odds of CDI hospitalization.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: No randomized trial defines the optimal regimen for fulminant CDI; current practice favors vancomycin (oral or rectal) plus IV metronidazole, while observational data suggest IV tigecycline adjunct yields ~79% cure.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: A two‑step diagnostic algorithm—high‑sensitivity NAAT followed by toxin EIA—improves specificity and reduces unnecessary antibiotic use for colonization in ICU settings.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: For patients with ≥3 recurrent CDI episodes, fecal microbiota transplantation achieves 80–90% cure rates and is considered standard of care; live biotherapeutic products (RBX2660, SER‑109) are approved but cost >$10,000 and have lower efficacy.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Rigorous donor screening for EPEC, STEC, and ESBL‑producing E. coli is essential to prevent severe infections in immunocompromised recipients undergoing FMT.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Proton pump inhibitor use for stress ulcer prophylaxis in the ICU increases CDI risk; stewardship programs should target PPI deprescribing when clinically appropriate.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: In ICU CDI, APACHE II scores predict ~40% mortality, and 20.9% of cases present as fulminant disease; treatment response rates are 56% versus 62–75% in non‑ICU populations.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
pearl: Bezlotoxumab reduced 12‑week recurrence from 20.9% to 13.5% (absolute 7.4% reduction) in MODIFY I/II trials; its withdrawal removes this proven benefit.

source_paper: Clostridioides difficile infection in the critically ill: Recent advances and emerging challenges
author: Pipitone Giuseppe et al.
