source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: Tuberculous meningitis usually shows CSF protein >100 mg/dL, glucose <40 mg/dL, and a dynamic shift from neutrophil predominance early to lymphocyte predominance later; low glucose is a key diagnostic clue.

source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: EVD‑associated infection rates are 15–22 % with median onset at 14 days; Gram‑negative organisms carry >50 % mortality, so maintain strict aseptic technique and consider prophylactic antibiotics in high‑risk cases.

source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: Craniotomy surgical site infection rates range 2–20 %, rising to ~30 % for aneurysm clipping; early postoperative CSF analysis can detect infection before clinical signs.

source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: Normal CSF leukocyte count (0–5 cells/µL) is expected in adults; infants may have up to 20 cells/µL due to immature BCSFB; thresholds for concern should be age‑adjusted.

source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: In bacterial meningitis, CSF IL‑6 and TNF‑α levels are markedly elevated; while not routinely used, they correlate with severity and can guide prognosis.

source_paper: CSF Leukocytes in Intracranial Infections: Diagnostic, Mechanistic, and Therapeutic Insights for Neurocritical Care
author: Mengting Li et al.
pearl: When initiating empiric therapy for bacterial meningitis, dexamethasone should be given 10–15 min before the first antibiotic dose or concurrently; delayed administration reduces benefit.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Postoperative pulmonary complications (PPCs) in ILD patients carry a 19.5% 30‑day mortality versus 0.5% in patients without PPCs; thus any perioperative plan must prioritize PPC prevention.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Acute exacerbation of ILD after surgery occurs in 6.3% of cases and is associated with an 80% mortality rate; early identification and mitigation of risk factors are essential.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: The ARISCAT risk index underestimates PPC risk in ILD; augment it with FVC, DLCO, 6‑minute walk distance, supplemental oxygen requirement, and pulmonary hypertension status for accurate stratification.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Obstructive sleep apnea is present in 76.4% of IPF patients; routine preoperative screening (e.g., STOP‑BANG or polysomnography) is warranted in all ILD surgical candidates.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Perioperative continuation of antifibrotic agents (nintedanib or pirfenidone) is safe and may reduce the risk of postoperative acute exacerbation, though prospective data are pending.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Use lung‑protective ventilation during general anesthesia: tidal volume 6–8 mL/kg ideal body weight and plateau pressure ≤30 cm H₂O to minimize ventilator‑induced lung injury.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Maintain perioperative SpO₂ at 88–92% to limit absorptive atelectasis and oxidative stress; in pulmonary hypertension, balance hyperoxia against hypoxemia to avoid increased pulmonary vascular resistance.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Plan early extubation to high‑flow nasal cannula (HFNC) or bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) in high‑risk ILD patients; this strategy is non‑inferior to routine intubation for preventing reintubation.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Smoking cessation for ≥2 months before surgery reduces sputum volume and lowers PPC risk in ILD patients.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Postpone elective surgery until any recent respiratory tract infection has fully resolved to avoid exacerbating ILD.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: For patients on chronic glucocorticoids, continue the usual daily dose perioperatively; do not switch to stress dosing unless adrenal insufficiency is present.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Hold mycophenolate, azathioprine, and tacrolimus one week before surgery when feasible; schedule rituximab at the end of its dosing cycle to minimize immunosuppression.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: When safe, choose regional or neuraxial anesthesia over general anesthesia in ILD patients to reduce PPC risk by avoiding residual neuromuscular blockade and hypoventilation.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Each additional 60 minutes of operative time increases the risk of acute respiratory worsening by 3% (RR 1.03); thus, minimizing surgical duration is advised.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: In patients with bibasilar fibrosis, avoid high PEEP because it raises pneumothorax risk and is linked to higher mortality; use the lowest PEEP that maintains oxygenation.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: In pulmonary hypertension, preferentially use norepinephrine over high‑dose phenylephrine to prevent unopposed pulmonary vasoconstriction and worsening right‑ventricular afterload.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Maintain intraoperative euvolemia; higher fluid balance is associated with postoperative acute exacerbation in IPF, so judicious IV fluid administration is critical.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Preoperative evaluation of ILD patients should include spirometry, DLCO, 6‑minute walk test, and transthoracic echocardiography to assess pulmonary function and cardiac status.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Cardiology assessment is recommended for all ILD surgical candidates, especially those with pulmonary hypertension, as surgery should be performed at centers with experienced PH expertise.

source_paper: Care of the Patient with Interstitial Lung Disease Perioperatively
author: Kristin Berger et al.
pearl: Patients with antiphospholipid syndrome should minimize periods off anticoagulation perioperatively to reduce thrombotic risk; bridging strategies should be individualized.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: In ARDS patients on controlled ventilation, a fluid challenge of 250–500 mL (colloid or crystalloid) is the standard volume used to test fluid responsiveness; a ≥15 % rise in cardiac output defines a positive response.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Unadjusted pulse pressure variation (PPV) predicts fluid responsiveness with an AUC of 0.67–0.85 in supine ARDS but drops to 0.49–0.85 in prone positioning; adjusting PPV for driving pressure or chest wall elastance improves discrimination in selected cohorts.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Stroke volume variation (SVV) shows moderate predictive value (AUC 0.75–0.79) in supine ARDS but is unreliable (AUC ≈0.5) during venovenous ECMO, likely due to altered preload dynamics.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Passive leg‑raising (PLR) induced stroke‑volume changes had good diagnostic accuracy in a single VV‑ECMO study, suggesting PLR may still be useful when fluid challenge is contraindicated, but evidence is limited.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Expiratory end‑obstetric tidal volume (EEOT) predicts fluid responsiveness with high accuracy (AUC 0.90–0.96) in ARDS, especially at higher PEEP levels, but requires specialized monitoring.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: The Vena cava thoracica (VTC) index achieved sensitivity and specificity >0.85 in one ARDS cohort, but performance varied in another, indicating that VTC may be context‑dependent.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: ΔCCI TREND (change in central venous pressure trend) demonstrated excellent discrimination (AUC 0.90) for fluid responsiveness in ARDS, but its use is limited by the need for continuous CVP monitoring.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Echocardiographic indices such as ΔSVC, ΔIVC, and ΔVmaxAO yield modest predictive accuracy (AUC 0.55–0.76) in ARDS, underscoring the need for multimodal assessment.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: No studies have evaluated fluid responsiveness predictors in spontaneously breathing ARDS patients, leaving a critical knowledge gap for the growing population of non‑intubated patients.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Fluid tolerance—assessed by extravascular lung water or venous congestion—has not been integrated into current predictive models; clinicians should evaluate these markers before administering fluids to avoid pulmonary edema or RV overload.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: The FACTT trial demonstrated that a conservative fluid strategy reduces ventilator days and improves lung mechanics in ARDS, supporting cautious fluid administration guided by responsiveness and tolerance.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: ARDS subphenotypes (e.g., hyperinflammatory vs. hypoinflammatory) may respond differently to fluid therapy; future protocols should stratify patients by subphenotype to individualize fluid management.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: High PEEP, prone positioning, and VV‑ECMO alter intrathoracic pressure dynamics, which can invalidate assumptions underlying dynamic indices like PPV and SVV; clinicians should interpret these indices with caution in such settings.

source_paper: Fluid responsiveness in ARDS: current evidence, knowledge gaps, and future directions — a scoping review of the literature
author: Alvarado-Sánchez J.I. et al.
pearl: Right ventricular dysfunction is common in ARDS; a positive PPV may indicate left‑sided fluid responsiveness while the RV is already volume‑stressed, potentially leading to RV overload.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: Mechanical insufflation–exsufflation (MI‑E) delivered at symmetric pressures of ±50 cmH₂O increases secretion clearance and improves static compliance (p = 0.005) in mechanically ventilated adults; higher pressures yield greater benefit.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: PEEP–ZEEP manoeuvres generate expiratory flow bias most consistently when performed during volume‑controlled ventilation; pressure‑support ventilation is less effective at creating this bias.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: Performing expiratory rib‑cage compression (ERCC) immediately before suctioning can raise sputum yield, and a multimodal protocol that includes ERCC reduced ventilator‑associated pneumonia (VAP) (p = 0.01).

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: Manual or ventilator hyperinflation (VHI/MHI) consistently increases secretion clearance compared with suction alone, with similar effectiveness between manual and ventilator‑assisted techniques.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: Current evidence shows no consistent effect of airway clearance techniques on mortality, duration of mechanical ventilation, or ICU length of stay; they should be used as adjuncts within a broader respiratory management plan.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: No major adverse events (barotrauma, accidental extubation) were reported, but safety data are inconsistent; monitor patients closely when applying high‑pressure techniques.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: When using PEEP–ZEEP or ventilator hyperinflation, volume‑controlled ventilation is preferred over pressure‑support to achieve a reliable expiratory flow bias.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: Integrate airway clearance techniques with sedation protocols and lung‑protective ventilation strategies; avoid excessive tidal volumes that may negate clearance benefits.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: The certainty of evidence for all outcomes is low to very low; clinical decisions should be individualized and based on bedside judgment rather than high‑level guideline mandates.

source_paper: Physiological and clinical effects of selected airway clearance techniques in mechanically ventilated adult ICU patients: a systematic review and synthesis without meta-analysis
author: Rossi V. et al.
pearl: In patients at high risk of extubation failure, MI‑E shows promise, but symmetric pressures used in studies differ from typical asymmetric protocols; tailor pressure settings to individual tolerance and response.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: In patients with suspected HFpEF, a normal BNP/NT‑proBNP does not exclude HF; if clinical suspicion remains high, proceed to invasive hemodynamic assessment.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Stage B (pre‑HF) patients with elevated natriuretic peptides or troponin, or structural abnormalities should be started on guideline‑directed medical therapy (ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta‑blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) to reduce progression to symptomatic HF by ~30‑40%.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce heart‑failure hospitalization by ~30‑40% in high‑risk patients with diabetes or CKD; GLP‑1 receptor agonists lower HF risk by ~10‑15%; finerenone decreases HF hospitalization by ~20% in CKD stage 3‑4.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: The lower limit of normal LVEF is 53% for women and 52% for men; an LVEF < 50% is considered abnormal regardless of sex, age, or ethnicity.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Classify HF into reduced, preserved, or improved EF rather than rigid numeric cutoffs; this classification directs therapy—HFrEF receives ACEi/ARNI, beta‑blocker, MRA, SGLT2i, while HFpEF focuses on blood‑pressure control and comorbidity management.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Use the universal cause classification (ischemic, hypertensive, valvular, arrhythmia‑related, infiltrative, toxic, heritable, etc.) to guide targeted treatment; for example, infiltrative cardiomyopathy requires disease‑specific therapy such as iron chelation for hemochromatosis.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Patients with HF with improved EF remain at risk for recurrence; continue guideline‑directed therapy and schedule periodic LVEF and biomarker monitoring every 6–12 months.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Worsening HF is defined by progressive symptom and QoL deterioration; decompensated HF requires escalation of therapy (e.g., diuretic intensification, combination diuretics, or advanced interventions).

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Screen asymptomatic high‑risk adults with BNP/NT‑proBNP; a level >125 pg/mL in men or >100 pg/mL in women suggests HF and warrants further evaluation.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: When evaluating suspected HF, systematically rule out CAD, CKD, pregnancy, and obesity as mimics; these conditions can present with dyspnea but are not driven by myocardial neurohumoral activation.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: In low‑ and middle‑income countries, hypertensive heart disease and rheumatic heart disease are the leading HF causes; prioritize screening and early treatment of hypertension and rheumatic fever to prevent HF.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Stage A patients (e.g., hypertension, diabetes, ASCVD) benefit from early initiation of ACEi/ARB/ARNI, beta‑blocker, MRA, and SGLT2i to lower HF incidence by up to 30%.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Symptomatic HF is considered permanent; even with LVEF recovery, maintain lifelong GDMT and surveillance to prevent relapse.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Stage D patients with recurrent hospitalizations despite GDMT should be evaluated for advanced therapies such as LVAD or heart transplant.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: Social determinants such as poverty, low education, and neighborhood deprivation significantly increase HF risk; incorporate social risk screening into HF risk stratification and management plans.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: HF trajectories—improvement, remission, recovery—should be defined by both functional and biomarker status; remission requires normalized LVEF, minimal symptoms, and stable biomarkers, whereas recovery requires sustained normalization over extended follow‑up.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: An LVEF improvement alone does not signify disease resolution; ongoing structural abnormalities and neurohumoral activation may persist, necessitating continued GDMT.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: In acute presentations, precipitating events such as MI, hypertensive emergency, arrhythmia, or pulmonary embolism should be promptly identified and treated to prevent progression to chronic HF.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: For patients with Stage B HF, a BNP/NT‑proBNP >100 pg/mL (women) or >125 pg/mL (men) or troponin elevation warrants initiation of GDMT even if asymptomatic.

source_paper: AHA/ACC/ESC/WHF Expert Consensus Document: Second Universal Definition of Heart Failure (2026)
pearl: HF with improved EF should be managed as HFrEF until EF normalizes; once EF >50% and symptoms resolve, consider de‑escalation of neurohumoral blockers only after 6 months of stability.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Initiate aggressive crystalloid resuscitation within the first 24 h of rhabdomyolysis, delivering >6 L/day (or ≥3 mL/kg/h) of lactated Ringer’s or normal saline to maintain urine output 1–3 mL/kg/h and prevent myoglobinuric AKI.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: A serum myoglobin level >4,000 ng/mL predicts AKI more accurately than CK; CK >1,000 IU/L confirms rhabdomyolysis, and CK >5,000 IU/L signals high AKI risk.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Adding sodium bicarbonate or mannitol to crystalloid therapy offers no significant benefit in preventing renal failure or reducing mortality compared with saline alone.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Initiate kidney replacement therapy for standard AKI indications—refractory hyperkalemia >6.5 mmol/L, pH <7.2, or BUN >40 mmol/L—rather than for myoglobin clearance alone.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Use continuous kidney replacement therapy (CKRT) in hemodynamically unstable patients; regional citrate anticoagulation extends filter life more effectively than heparin.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Urinary TIMP‑2 × IGFBP7 ≥0.3 (ng/mL)² predicts moderate‑to‑severe AKI within 12 h with high sensitivity; routine use is limited by cost.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Loop diuretics such as furosemide are not recommended for preventing or treating AKI in rhabdomyolysis; use only to manage fluid overload.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Maintain MAP >65 mmHg (or >70–75 mmHg in chronic hypertension) and renal perfusion pressure ≥60 mmHg; norepinephrine is the preferred vasopressor in AKI.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Treat hyperkalemia >6 mmol/L with cardiac monitoring, ECG, and insulin‑glucose; refractory hyperkalemia >6.5 mmol/L warrants urgent KRT.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: Treat hypocalcemia only if symptomatic or with severe hyperkalemia; early calcium replacement risks deposition in injured muscle.

source_paper: Critical care considerations in rhabdomyolysis-associated acute kidney injury and kidney replacement therapy
author: Wong FHA, See KC
pearl: A McMahon risk score ≥6 predicts need for KRT with 86% sensitivity and 68% specificity; CK ≥5,000 IU/L has 83% sensitivity but 55% specificity for KRT.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: The 5‑year risk of all‑cause death or heart transplantation in acute myocarditis is 4 % overall, rising to 16 % in patients with impaired LVEF or ventricular arrhythmias.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Cardiac magnetic resonance using the updated Lake Louise criteria has a sensitivity of 74–88 % and specificity of 86–92 % for acute myocarditis in non‑fulminant cases, comparable to endomyocardial biopsy.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Patients with acute heart failure, cardiogenic shock, ventricular arrhythmia, high‑degree AV block, or severely impaired LV output should be admitted to a Level 3 cardiac ICU; Level 2 ICU is indicated for STEMI‑like ECG, elevated hs‑cTn, or mild LV dysfunction; low‑risk patients may be managed on a regular ward.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: In fulminant myocarditis, performing an endomyocardial biopsy early (within the first 3–4 weeks) reduces all‑cause death/transplant (70 % vs 49 %, P = 0.004) compared with delayed biopsy.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Routine immunosuppressive therapy is not supported in virus‑negative lymphocytic myocarditis; the MYTHS trial is currently evaluating corticosteroids in this setting.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Moderate‑ to high‑intensity exercise should be avoided for at least 6 months if symptoms, elevated hs‑cTn, or significant ECG/imaging abnormalities persist, especially with LGE > 20 % and reduced LVEF.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: CMR should be performed 3–14 days after symptom onset; imaging too early (<3 days) or too late (>2 weeks) reduces diagnostic accuracy.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: The spatial distribution of late gadolinium enhancement on CMR is an independent prognostic marker for mortality in acute myocarditis.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Obtain at least 3–4 endomyocardial biopsy samples, preferably biventricular, for optimal diagnostic accuracy; complication rates are <1 % in high‑volume centers.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Absolute indications for EMB include recurrent myocarditis and persistent cardiac dysfunction; ESC/ACCF/AHA guidelines also recommend EMB for persistent de‑novo HF <2 weeks with abnormal LV, arrhythmias 2–12 weeks, high‑grade AV block, failure to respond to therapy within 1–2 weeks, peripheral eosinophilia, ICI therapy, or fulminant presentation.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: In patients with STEMI‑like presentation and normal coronary arteries, up to one‑third may have acute myocarditis diagnosed by systematic CMR.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Women have a 2.7‑fold increased risk of death or transplantation at 10 years compared with men, underscoring the need for sex‑specific risk stratification.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: ST‑segment changes are present in >66 % of acute myocarditis patients, and >50 % present with a STEMI‑like picture; consider myocarditis in young patients with STEMI‑like symptoms.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Baseline ECG and cardiac biomarkers have variable sensitivity; normal results do not exclude myocarditis.

source_paper: Clinical management of acute myocarditis in daily practice: an expert practical view
author: Mathieu Kerneis et al.
pearl: Acute myocarditis should be confirmed by positive endomyocardial biopsy (Dallas Criteria + immunohistochemistry) or CMR within the first 3–4 weeks of symptom onset.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Electrical storm is defined as ≥3 sustained VT/VF episodes within 24 h, each separated by ≥5 min, requiring ATP or shock termination.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: The STORM classification uses four bedside parameters—acute heart‑failure/instability, inotropes/vasopressors, deep sedation or renal replacement, and mechanical circulatory support—to assign stages 1–5, with 30‑day mortality rising from 5 % (Stage 1) to 50 % (Stage 5).

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Compared with STORM‑1, adjusted hazard ratios for 30‑day mortality are 3.96 (Stage 3), 6.62 (Stage 4), and 14.36 (Stage 5).

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: STEMI as the trigger occurs in 81.2 % of STORM‑5 patients versus 5.9 % in STORM‑1, underscoring its role in severe electrical storm.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: VF constitutes 57.1 % of arrhythmias in STORM‑5 versus 27.3 % in STORM‑1, indicating higher VF burden with advanced stages.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Amiodarone is administered in 93.8 % of STORM‑5 versus 78.4 % of STORM‑1, reflecting escalated antiarrhythmic therapy with severity.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Lidocaine use rises from 29.0 % in STORM‑1 to 87.5 % in STORM‑5, indicating increased reliance on IV antiarrhythmics in severe electrical storm.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Catheter ablation is performed in 43.2 % of STORM‑1 but only 6.2 % of STORM‑5, suggesting limited use in the most critical patients.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: Neuraxial modulation is applied in 25.0 % of STORM‑5 versus 1.0 % of STORM‑1, highlighting its role in refractory electrical storm.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: ECMO is employed in 93.8 % of STORM‑5 patients (overall 10.2 %), indicating its preferential use in the highest‑risk group.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: STORM‑5 patients are younger (median 54 y) with lower LVEF (25 %) and higher ischemic cardiomyopathy (87.5 %) compared to STORM‑1 (median 66 y, LVEF 30 %).

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: STORM staging provides a standardized framework for multidisciplinary communication and targeted resource allocation, analogous to SCAI shock staging but specific to electrical pathophysiology.

source_paper: A practical 5-stage clinical scale for electrical storm: the STORM classification
author: Raphaël Martins et al.
pearl: The STORM classification is derived retrospectively and lacks external validation; its parameters reflect treatment intensity as a surrogate for severity.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Place an external ventricular drain (EVD) early in TBI patients with post‑traumatic hydrocephalus, intraventricular hemorrhage, or neurological decline attributable to elevated ICP, but do not delay urgent life‑saving procedures such as hematoma evacuation or decompressive craniectomy.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Insert the EVD at Kocher’s point on the non‑dominant side, directing the catheter perpendicular to the calvarium toward the foramen of Monro; confirm depth by CSF return, tunnel the catheter ≥10 cm subcutaneously, and secure it to minimize displacement and infection.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Because EVDs provide less accurate continuous ICP monitoring than intraparenchymal sensors, combine EVD drainage with an intraparenchymal ICP probe when both CSF diversion and reliable ICP data are required.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Begin CSF drainage gradually, titrating to ICP trends; after ICP stabilization, choose continuous or intermittent drainage based on individual response, and evaluate catheter patency immediately if waveform pulsatility is absent.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Administer preoperative antibiotics for all EVD insertions; do not give routine postoperative prophylaxis or schedule catheter exchanges; remove the EVD as soon as clinically feasible to reduce infection risk.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Treat suspected EVD‑associated ventriculitis empirically with vancomycin plus a third‑generation cephalosporin or carbapenem; for MRSA use vancomycin alone, for MSSA use nafcillin or oxacillin, and for gram‑negative bacilli use ceftazidime, cefepime, or meropenem; continue therapy 10–14 days (extend to 21 days for MDR strains).

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Cisternostomy should be reserved for TBI patients with refractory intracranial hypertension and significant traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage; it is an adjunct, not a substitute, for decompressive craniectomy or CSF‑diversion procedures.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Avoid cisternostomy in patients with isolated diffuse axonal injury lacking intracranial hypertension or in those with anterior/middle fossa skull base fractures, as the risk of CSF leak outweighs potential benefit.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: Perform cisternostomy via a frontal approach, open the Liliequist membrane, and avoid anterior clinoidectomy; connect the patient to a closed sterile drainage system (ideally an EVD) with gravity drainage set 5–7 cm H₂O above the tragus, limiting catheter duration to ≤7 days.

source_paper: Recommendations for ventriculostomy and cisternostomy in TBI management: Results of the WFNS-Neurotraumatology committee “Cusco consensus”
pearl: All cisternostomy patients must be connected to a closed sterile drainage system, preferably an EVD, with gravity-based drainage at 5–7 cm H₂O above the tragus, and the catheter should not remain in place longer than 7 days to minimize infection.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Pulmonary hypertension is defined by right heart catheterization as a mean pulmonary arterial pressure (mPAP) >20 mmHg; patients with mPAP 21–24 mmHg are not automatically treated.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Low- or intermediate-risk PAH (WHO‑FC I/II, 6MWD > 440 m, RAP < 8 mmHg, CI ≥ 2.5 L/min/m², mPAP ≤ 40 mmHg) should start oral ERA (ambrisentan or macitentan) plus PDE5i (tadalafil) at diagnosis.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: High-risk PAH (any parameter outside low‑risk set) requires immediate triple therapy: parenteral prostacyclin (epoprostenol or treprostinil) plus ERA and PDE5i.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: In CTEPH patients unsuitable for pulmonary endarterectomy, balloon pulmonary angioplasty is a Class I recommendation and can normalize mPAP and PVR.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Low‑risk acute PE (sPESI = 0, no RV dysfunction) may be managed outpatient with a DOAC and early discharge within 24–48 h.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: High‑risk PE with shock requires systemic thrombolysis first‑line; surgical embolectomy or catheter‑directed therapy are alternatives if thrombolysis is contraindicated or fails.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Extended anticoagulation is recommended for unprovoked VTE, cancer‑associated VTE, and recurrent VTE; DOACs are preferred over warfarin in non‑cancer patients due to lower major‑bleeding risk.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: In cancer‑associated VTE, edoxaban or rivaroxaban are effective; the ONCO‑DVT trial shows 12‑month edoxaban is superior to 3‑month for isolated distal DVT.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: IVC filters should be placed only when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated in acute proximal DVT or PE, and must be retrieved once bleeding risk resolves.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Early ambulation and compression stockings reduce pain and edema in acute DVT but do not prevent post‑thrombotic syndrome.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Inhaled nitric oxide can acutely lower pulmonary artery pressure in massive PE but is limited to hemodynamic stabilization as a bridge to definitive therapy.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: A V/Q scan is the critical initial test to rule out CTEPH because of its high sensitivity; a normal scan essentially excludes CTEPH.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Right‑heart catheterization is essential for definitive PH diagnosis, classification, and severity assessment and guides therapy decisions.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Vasoreactivity testing is recommended only in idiopathic, heritable, or drug‑induced PAH to identify candidates for high‑dose calcium channel blockers.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: A positive vasoreactivity test is defined as a drop in mPAP ≥10 mmHg to ≤40 mmHg with stable or increased cardiac output.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Genetic testing and counseling are recommended for all PAH patients, especially for BMPR2 mutations, as they influence prognosis and family screening.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: In pediatric PAH, risk stratification must incorporate BNP/NT‑proBNP, uric acid, and imaging findings to guide treatment escalation.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: For intermediate‑risk PAH on dual oral therapy, adding selexipag or inhaled treprostinil should be considered to improve outcomes.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: In PAH patients with cardiopulmonary comorbidities, initial monotherapy is preferred to avoid polypharmacy and adverse interactions.

source_paper: JCS/JPCPHS 2025 Guideline on Pulmonary Hypertension and Pulmonary Embolism/Deep Vein Thrombosis
pearl: Adding sotatercept to PAH therapy improves 6‑minute walk distance and is considered for intermediate‑ or high‑risk patients during follow‑up.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Universal anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is not recommended for liver, kidney, or pancreas transplant recipients; evidence shows no benefit (risk difference 0.1%) and potential harm (non-serious adverse events increased by 6.7%).

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Targeted anti-Aspergillus prophylaxis is recommended only for high‑risk liver transplant recipients—those with peri‑transplant RRT, re‑transplantation, or fulminant hepatic failure—when baseline IA incidence is ≥5%.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: In high‑risk liver transplant patients, targeted prophylaxis reduces IA incidence by 13.7% (95% CI –15.9 to –5.8%).

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Preferred prophylactic agents for high‑risk liver transplant recipients are echinocandins (micafungin, anidulafungin, caspofungin) or newer mold‑active triazoles (voriconazole, posaconazole, isavuconazole); avoid amphotericin B and itraconazole.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Echinocandins have breakthrough IA rates of 0–3% and minimal drug‑drug interactions, but lack activity against Cryptococcus and Rhizopus.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Mold‑active triazoles have breakthrough IFI rates <3% but require therapeutic drug monitoring of calcineurin inhibitors due to significant drug interactions.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Amphotericin B formulations are associated with nephrotoxicity and serious adverse events leading to discontinuation in 5–13% of liver transplant recipients.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Itraconazole is limited by unreliable absorption and causes non‑serious GI adverse events in 24.3% of recipients.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: The optimal duration of targeted prophylaxis in liver transplant recipients is until hospital discharge or up to 28 days post‑transplant.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: In kidney transplant recipients, pooled IA incidence is 0.4–0.8%; universal prophylaxis is not supported due to low incidence.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: In pancreas transplant recipients, IA incidence is 0.8% with onset 90–340 days post‑transplant; universal prophylaxis is not supported.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Risk factors for IA in kidney transplant recipients include prolonged pre‑transplant RRT, older age, preceding bacterial pneumonia, diabetes, and leukopenia.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: In pancreas transplant recipients, IA cases have a one‑year all‑cause mortality of 70%, but only 3 deaths are directly attributable to IA.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: When using mold‑active triazoles in liver transplant recipients, monitor calcineurin inhibitor levels closely to avoid subtherapeutic immunosuppression.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Echinocandins are preferred over amphotericin B in liver transplant recipients due to lower nephrotoxicity and minimal drug interactions.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: In high‑risk liver transplant patients, the baseline IA incidence of 16.9% justifies targeted prophylaxis, whereas in general liver transplant recipients the incidence is 1.9%.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Universal prophylaxis in liver transplant recipients yields a risk difference of 0.1% for IA, with no statistically significant benefit, and increases non‑serious adverse events by 6.7%.

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: Targeted prophylaxis in high‑risk liver transplant recipients reduces IA incidence by 13.7% (95% CI –15.9 to –5.8%).

source_paper: 2026 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines on Prevention of Invasive Aspergillosis in Adult Solid Organ Transplant Recipients – Liver, Kidney and Pancreas Transplant Recipients
pearl: For liver transplant recipients requiring prophylaxis, echinocandins provide a favorable safety profile and activity against Candida spp., making them a suitable first‑line option.

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Early palliative care consultation is recommended for all ECMO patients at the time of ECMO consult; it improves symptom control and goal‑concordant care without increasing mortality (consensus panel recommendation).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Routine clinical ethics consultation should occur within 72 h of cannulation and at 2‑ and 4‑week intervals to proactively mitigate moral distress and support communication (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: An interdisciplinary ECMO consult team—including an intensivist, ECMO proceduralist, ethicist, and palliative care consultant—and a designated “contrarian” member should be assembled to reduce cognitive bias and ensure equitable selection (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Standardized documentation for all ECMO consults, including decision‑makers, severity of illness, treatments, and reasoning, should be used to promote transparency and facilitate quality improvement (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: When ECMO is judged non‑beneficial (e.g., no exit strategy, no recovery milestones met), an interdisciplinary meeting should be convened to recommend compassionate discontinuation (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Patients should be informed that inclusion in an outcomes registry may influence care decisions and that they have the right to withdraw consent at any time (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: If a patient with capacity explicitly decides to stop ECMO, the care team must honor that decision without delay (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Programs should develop policies to manage competing interests, ensuring patient‑centered values override institutional pressures such as transplant program benchmarking metrics (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Quality improvement reviews of ECMO candidacy decisions should be performed periodically for both cannulated and non‑cannulated patients to identify variability and improve outcomes (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: ECMO should be framed as a temporary, time‑limited intervention with a clear early communication about the possibility of non‑recovery to set realistic expectations (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: If specialist palliative care is limited, ICU clinicians can expand primary palliative care delivery through targeted training and mentorship to provide symptom management and communication support (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Early palliative care consults are not associated with increased mortality in ECMO patients, countering the misconception that they equate to hospice (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Less than 50 % of ECMO patients receive a palliative care consult, highlighting a gap in care that can be addressed by early referral (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: When ECMO has no exit strategy and recovery milestones are not met, the team should consider discontinuation rather than prolonging therapy that offers no benefit (consensus panel).

source_paper: Ethical Challenges of Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery Cardiac Society Consensus on Key Issues and Practical Recommendations
pearl: Programs should adopt a stepwise conflict resolution pathway based on multi‑society policy statements for intractable disagreements about ECMO continuation (consensus panel).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: In an unstable patient with high suspicion for pneumothorax, the absence of lung sliding, B‑lines, and lung pulse in the anterior‑inferior chest is an independent indication to proceed with immediate needle or chest tube drainage (SSC 2025, strong recommendation).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: A lung point located anterior to the mid‑axillary line corresponds to a small pneumothorax with approximately 15 % lung collapse, allowing semi‑quantification of the effusion size (2025 update, consensus).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: In acute undifferentiated respiratory failure, the absence of diffuse B‑lines on lung ultrasound rules out cardiogenic pulmonary edema with higher sensitivity than supine chest radiography (2025 update, strong recommendation).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: A low aeration score in the non‑dependent anterior chest regions on lung ultrasound reliably identifies the focal ARDS phenotype, guiding ventilatory strategy (2025 update, consensus).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: Persistent B‑lines at discharge in patients hospitalized for acute heart failure predict higher rates of rehospitalization and mortality; they should prompt intensified diuretic therapy or closer follow‑up (2025 update, evidence).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: Lung ultrasound‑guided assessment of pulmonary congestion in chronic hemodialysis patients can optimize ultrafiltration targets and reduce heart‑failure hospitalizations (2025 update, consensus).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: Estimate pleural effusion volume with V (ml) = D (mm) × 20, where D is the maximal pleural separation on a lateral transverse view; this simple formula correlates well with CT volume (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: B‑line detection is not significantly affected by variations in machine settings, probe type, or image adjustments; thus, clinical interpretation remains consistent across platforms (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: For B‑line visualization, use a single focal point on the pleural line, harmonics off, spatial compound off, depth 10–12 cm (up to 18 cm in obesity), gain increased in the far field, and a probe perpendicular to the pleura (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: Visualization of the thoracic spine above the diaphragm (spine sign) on lung ultrasound is highly accurate for confirming pleural effusion or lower‑lobe consolidation (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: The combination of absent lung sliding, absent B‑lines, absent lung pulse, and presence of a lung point constitutes the sonographic diagnosis of pneumothorax (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: In hydropneumothorax, look for air‑bubble artifacts within the effusion and the hydropoint—the interface where fluid meets absent sliding (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: If a lung point is not visualized in a very large pneumothorax, confirm the diagnosis with chest X‑ray or CT, as ultrasound may miss the air‑air interface (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: Lung ultrasound has the same high specificity as chest X‑ray for confirming pneumothorax but is more accurate for ruling it out, especially in supine patients (2025 update).

source_paper: International evidence-based recommendations for point-of-care lung ultrasound: 2025 focused update of the 2012 recommendations
pearl: A positive zone for interstitial syndrome is defined by ≥3 B‑lines in a single frame; an 8‑zone anterolateral protocol is ideal, with 6‑zone or 4‑zone for acute respiratory failure and posterior zones added for interstitial lung disease (2025 update).

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: In septic shock, administer broad‑spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition; each hour delay reduces survival by 7.6% (SSC 2026, strong recommendation).

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: For non‑shock sepsis, avoid blanket rapid broad‑spectrum coverage; perform a rapid evaluation within 3–5 hours (IDSA) to reduce unnecessary exposure and harm.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Antibiotic de‑escalation within 24 h of definitive culture results is safe and reduces mortality (RR 0.68, 95% CI 0.52–0.88); multiplex PCR RDTs can enable ADE within 5 h thanks to 92–99% NPV.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Short‑course therapy is non‑inferior and safer: ≤5–7 days for community‑acquired pneumonia, ≤8 days for HAP/VAP, and 4–7 days for intra‑abdominal infection after source control.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Avoid empiric anti‑anaerobic agents in critically ill patients unless clearly indicated; they are associated with reduced survival (HR 1.14, 95% CI 1.02–1.28).

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Selective digestive decontamination may lower mortality (RR 0.91) but is not recommended in ICUs with high MDR rates; evidence is mixed and may promote resistance.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Use procalcitonin‑guided stewardship to shorten empiric therapy and lower mortality; algorithms have shown reduced duration and improved outcomes.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Do not routinely add aminoglycosides to beta‑lactams; combination therapy offers no survival benefit and increases nephrotoxicity, except for highly resistant GNB, endocarditis, or necrotizing fasciitis.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Empiric coverage for a specific pathogen is justified only when local prevalence exceeds 10%; otherwise, narrow coverage reduces unnecessary exposure.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Machine‑learning antibiogram tools can eliminate unnecessary MRSA coverage in ~69% of cases, reducing broad‑spectrum use without compromising outcomes.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Prolonged beta‑lactam infusions are not routinely superior; use them only for high‑risk subgroups (e.g., augmented renal clearance, high MIC organisms) as MERCY/BLING III showed no overall mortality benefit.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Therapeutic drug monitoring is recommended for glycopeptides and aminoglycosides to avoid toxicity; routine TDM for beta‑lactams has not improved clinical outcomes.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Single‑dose IV ceftriaxone in acute brain injury reduces early VAP (HR 0.60) and increases ICU‑free days; consider prophylactic ceftriaxone in this population.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Inhaled prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., amikacin) lower VAP incidence but do not improve mortality or ICU length of stay; use only when VAP prevention is a priority.

source_paper: Optimal antibiotic use in the intensive care unit
author: Scott T. Micek et al.
pearl: Audit prophylactic antibiotic use; inappropriate prescribing occurs up to 50% of the time and durations often exceed twice the recommended length, contributing to resistance.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: In septic shock patients with moderate endotoxin activity (EAA 0.6–0.89) and significant organ dysfunction (SOFA > 11 or MODS > 9), polymyxin B hemoperfusion (PMX‑HP) shows a survival signal when started within 24 h of shock onset (TIGRIS, preliminary data).

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: Exclude patients with EAA < 0.6 from PMX‑HP, as endotoxin is unlikely to drive their shock, and exclude those with EAA > 0.9, where overwhelming infection may blunt hemoperfusion benefit (TIGRIS inclusion criteria).

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: The Endotoxin Activity Assay (EAA) quantifies neutrophil respiratory‑burst priming and correlates with severity and mortality; use EAA to identify endotoxin‑driven sepsis rather than relying on Gram‑negative culture alone.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: Early PMX‑HP (within 6 h of abdominal source control) improves hemodynamics and confers a mortality benefit in abdominal septic shock per the EUPHAS trial; timing is essential for efficacy.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: Large trials (ABDOMIX, EUPHRATES) that enrolled septic patients without endotoxin enrichment reported neutral mortality, highlighting the necessity of biological enrichment to detect benefit.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: Activated protein C improved survival in PROWESS but failed in PROWESS‑SHOCK and was withdrawn due to bleeding risk, illustrating that coagulation‑targeted therapies can harm when applied to patients without coagulation activation.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
author: Toshiaki Iba et al.
pearl: Precision sepsis care should integrate multiple dynamic biomarkers (endotoxin, coagulation, immune) to guide rational combination therapy; monotherapy is unlikely to address the multidimensional pathophysiology.

source_paper: Precision medicine in sepsis: lessons from the TIGRIS trial and the reawakening of targeted therapy
